1: Life cycle assessment of biological pig manure treatment versus direct land application - a trade-off story
L. Corbala-Robles 1 , W.N.D. Sastafiana , V. Van lindenc, E.I.P. Volcke T. Schaubroeck

a Department of Biosystems Engineering, Ghent University, Coupure Links 653, B-9000, Ghent, Belgium b Research group Environmental Organic Chemistry and Technology (EnVOC), Faculty of Bioscience Engineering, Ghent University, Coupure Links 653, B-9000, Ghent, Belgium C Flanders Research Institute for Agriculture, Fisheries and Food (ILVO), Burgemeester Van Gansberghelaan 115, B-9820, Merelbeke, Belgium d RDI Unit Life Cycle Sustainability and Risk Assessment (LiSRA), Department of Environmental Research & Innovation (ERIN), Luxembourg Institute of Science and Technology (LIST), 41 Rue du Brill, L-4422, Belvaux, Luxembourg

R T I C INFO|ABSTRACT Keywords: Pig manure Biological treatment Modelling Life cycle assessment Sustainability ReCiPe|Excess manure can have two common fates: to be exported and applied to agricultural land, or to be treated, possibly with resource recovery (i.e. energy and/or nutrients). In this study, the environmental performance of a treatment system of pig manure (centrifugation and subsequent biological nitrogen removal from the liquid fraction and composting of the solid fraction) has been assessed using life cycle assessment (LCA) with the ReCiPe method to assess environmental impacts at midpoint and endpoint level. Such treatment system is typical for Flanders (Belgium), a region characterized by a manure excess. The performance of this treatment-scenario has been compared to the direct field application of untreated manure (no-treatment-scenario) to gain insight in the environmental trade-offs between them. The hotspots dominating the environmental impact for manure treatment were the field application of compost and the effluent from the biological treatment, and the electricity needed to run the treatment facility. The substitution of synthetic fertilizers played an important role in both scenarios (mitigation of potential damaging impacts). The comparison between the two manure management scenarios showed that the treatment scenario scores better in some categories and vice versa. Manure treatment does prevent marine eutrophication and must be carried out in nitrate-vulnerable zones, such as the studied region of Flanders. Finally, the use of single score through normalization and weighting of midpoint impacts was evaluated. This underscores a policy direction towards manure treatment, but this message should be interpreted with care as the approach of normalization and aggregation can be questioned.
2: The rapidly increasing world population and limited agricultural land have considerably driven an increase and intensification of the global livestock production since the 1960s (Prapaspongsa et al., 2010; Overloop et al., 2001). In 2014 Europe had an important contribution to the world meat production of chicken (17%), cattle (16%) and pig (24%), (FAOSTAT, 2017). This production is distributed across Europe with areas of higher production intensities. Traditionally, manure was applied as fertilizer to agricultural land and provided input of nutrients, which yielded crops and grass. Nowadays, there is a surplus of nutrients in areas with intensive livestock production and limited agricultural land as a result of excess manure production. This excess needs to be managed.

Excess manure production in a region is usually managed in three ways: i) exported and applied to agricultural land; ii) nutrient removal from the manure; iii) conversion of nutrients into mineral fertilizer. Flanders, Belgium, has regions that reach a total manure production equivalent to 340 kg N per ha (Jacobsen, 2015), and has been desig- nated as a 100% Nitrate Vulnerable Zone according to the European Nitrate Directive (EC/91/676). That directive limits fertilisation from manure sources to 170 kg N per ha. In 2014, the manure production in Flanders reached 125 million kg N (VLM, 2015). That same year in Flanders, there was a manure management capacity (including ex- portation) of around 39.3 million kg N. Pig manure encompassed 44% of this total (VCM, 2014).
Life cycle assessment (LCA) allows one to assess the environmental sustainability of a product or service (e.g. manure management) over its

complete life cycle (ISO, 2006a,b). In practice, the representation of the impact can be assessed at an early stage of the cause-effect chain, called the midpoint level, or as the final effect on areas of protection (AoP) at an endpoint level (De Meester, 2013). Indicators are used to express this damage, e.g. disability adjusted life years (DALY; i.e. loss in healthy life years) as endpoint indicator for damage to human health or kg CO2 equivalents as midpoint indicator for the effect of greenhouse gas emissions on climate change. Ideally, without considering practical limitations, endpoint indicators should be preferred, as these cover the complete cause-effect chain up to the AoPs (Schaubroeck and Rugani, 2017). However, in practice, midpoint indicators are often considered or advised instead of endpoint indicators due to the higher reliability and availability of data and models (Hauschild et al., 2013).

LCA has been used to assess different pig manure management scenarios in different geographical locations. Prapaspongsa et al. (2010) identified environmental impacts from twelve different sce- narios in Denmark, changing the type of treatment, storage and land application systems. De Vries et al. (2012) assessed the consequences of a manure processing technology that separates the manure into a solid and liquid fraction and de-waters the liquid fraction using reverse os- mosis (in the Netherlands). Lopez-Ridaura et al. (2009) compared the environmental performance of the biological treatment of manure to its direct transportation and injection into crop land (in Bretagne, France). These studies calculated the environmental impacts at midpoint and used up to five impact categories to make their analyses. Brockmann et al. (2014) also compared the environmental performance of the biological treatment of manure to its direct application to the field, and took into account 18 midpoint impact categories in their discussion (in Bretagne, France). Endpoint impact was also calculated, but not dis- cussed in the article. De Vries et al. (2015) assessed the trade-offs be- tween the North West European practice, the Dutch current situation of progressive manure management and the application of a new in- tegrated manure management practice applied to the European one. They paid particular attention to avoid pollution swapping within the manure management system. An overview of these works is shown in Table 1. Furthermore, a thorough chronological review of LCA studies of pig production was carried out by McAuliffe et al. (2016). In their study, LCA evaluations of pig production were studied that covered three life cycle stages: feed production, entire-system livestock rearing, and waste management.

This study is looking into the environmental performance and trade- offs between two manure management scenarios within the context of Flanders, Belgium, a nitrate vulnerable zone. The treatment-scenario comprises centrifugation and subsequent biological nitrogen removal from the liquid fraction, which is the most applied manure processing technique in Flanders, Belgium -80 out of 120 installations in 2015 (VLM, 2015). The no-treatment-scenario comprises the transportation and field application of untreated manure. This comparison will be

done through the analysis of 18 midpoint categories, pinpointing of hotspots through endpoint impact assessment, assessing the sensitivity to the selection of background processes and discussing the validity of single-score impact assessment.

7: The goal of this study was to construct an inventory and evaluate the potential environmental impacts of a initrification/denitrification biological pig manure treatment plant in a nitrate-vulnerable zone Flanders, Belgium. The environmental impact evaluation was carried out using the LCA framework according to the ISO 14040/14044 guidelines (ISO, 2006a,b). The environmental impacts from this manure management option (treatment scenario) were then compared to the ones of the direct field application of untreated manure (no-treatment scenario). The comparison provides transparent information for dif- ferent stakeholders regarding the environmental trade-offs between those two scenarios for managing the excess nitrogen from pig manure. An overview of the scenarios inputs, outputs, foreground system and background system is presented in Fig. 1.

As foreground system for the treatment-scenario, the manure man- agement system considered is described. When arriving to the treat- ment facility, raw manure is pumped into an underground storage as soon as it arrives. Then, the raw manure undergoes centrifugation. The thin fraction is sent to the biological treatment, while the thick fraction is transported to a composting facility. Finally, the biological effluent and the compost are transported and applied to agricultural fields. In the no-treatment scenario, the foreground system describes raw manure storage, and transport and spreading to agricultural land. In the back- ground systems of both scenarios (see Section 2.2.4), impacts outside the manure management systems considered have been accounted for e.g. electricity production, manure transport to the treatment fa- cility, chemical production (Fig. 1). The main input to the system is raw manure. Emissions to air and soil are the main outputs.

The Functional Unit (FU) used was "1 m³ of raw manure" since the final output generated in each scenario is different and volume is the most commonly used FU for wastewater treatment plant (WWTP) analysis (Corominas et al., 2013).

0: SYSTEM BOUNDARY OF THE NO-TREATMENT SCENARIO

Fig. 1. System boundaries for the life cycle inventory of the considered scenarios. The foreground system is indicated by a grey box, including the processes, framed in bold, that produce the main product and emissions. The processes from the background system are indicated by a dashed box.

relevant previous measurements. For the background system, data from the LCI ecoinvent version 3 database was used (Swiss Centre for Life Cycle Inventories). The main system input, manure, is considered a waste and to waste no environmental impact (of their generation) is attributed (ISO, 2006a), implying a so called "cut off" approach. In accordance, the "cut off" variant of ecoinvent 3.2 (Wernet et al., 2016) was used. However, for coproducts of the manure treatment process (e.g. composted solid fraction of manure), the impact of the avoided substitute (e.g. mineral fertilizer in this case) was subtracted (see Sec- tion 2.2.6 and Appendix B in Supplementary material). Heimersson et al. (2017) after all argue that for life cycle modelling of WWTP, the latter approach is most readily applicable.

2.2.1. Characteristics of the raw pig manure and manure-based products For the liquid fraction after centrifugation and effluent from the biological treatment, the density was assumed similar to pig urine as reported by Backhurst and Harker (1974). The percentage of dry matter (DM) for the solid and liquid fractions were derived from Moller et al. (2002). Dry matter values for compost were obtained from Brockmann et al. (2014). It was assumed that 90% of the nitrogen and 7.5% of the phosphorus ends up in the liquid fraction (Hjorth et al., 2010; Smet et al., 2003; Moller et al., 2000). The raw manure characteristics were mainly taken from ASAE (2003) and derivations from values given by Corbala Robles et al. (2016).
The solid fraction was assumed to be processed in a tunnel

5: <header>2.2.2. Biological treatment process description and mathematical modelling </header>

The thin fraction of manure is treated biologically in a nitrification- denitrification sequencing batch reactor (SBR) system (80 m³ per day). Methanol is added as an additional carbon source to adjust the chemical oxygen demand (COD) and nitrogen ratio (COD:N). The mathematical model describing the nitrification-denitrification processes was based on the work of Volcke (2006) and implemented in MatLab Simulink This model consists of liquid phase mass balances over reactor for the following components: H+, ammonia, nitrite, nitrate, carbon dioxide, phosphate, organic matter (represented as COD in the model), oxygen, dinitrogen, methanol, ammonia oxidizing bacteria, nitrite oxidizing bacteria and heterotrophic bacteria. The pH is calculated as in Volcke (2006).
6: <header>2.2.3. Electricity consumption calculations </header>
2.2.3.1. Electricity consumption at the manure treatment plant. A detailed explanation of the electricity consumption calculations is presented in Appendix B in Supplementary material.

The electricity consumption for aeration in the nitrification tank is based on a fine bubble diffused aeration system as described in the biological manure treatment plant by Lemmens et al. (2007). The aeration efficiency is 3-6 kg O2 kWh (Smet et al., 2003). The power estimation for centrifugation was based on the value modelled by De Meester (2013) who is referring to the default power of different types of equipment reported by Perry and Green (1999). Based on that re- ference, a decanter centrifuge with a liquid throughput of 0.0315 r 1 has a typical motor size of 223.81 kW. For mixing of the denitrification reactor, the power estimation refers to the average power consumption per unit volume of the anoxic reactor (Deni- trification tank) of 0.008 kWh.m as described by Tchobanoglous et al. (2003).

An electricity consumption of 16.9 kWh.FU-1 was obtained. Different electricity consumption values can be found in literature. Brockmann et al. (2014) reported 14.5 kWh.FU-1 in their LCA study, Lopez-Ridaura et al. (2009) mentioned 18.7 kWh.FU for the cen- trifuge, aeration and pumping as observed in the work of Levasseur

<list>(2005) and a consumption of around 17 kWh.FU was obtained in a survey in Britanny, France (Béline et al., 2004). Aeration in the biolo- gical reactor, followed by centrifugation, were the main sources of electricity consumption in this and the cited studies. 
2.2.3.2. Electricity consumption for the composting process. The energy use for the large scale tunnel composting installation was assumed to be 45-50 kWh/ton (Lemmens et al., 2007), including pre- and post- treatment, conversion and aeration. 
2.2.4. Background processes 
2.2.4.1. Electricity production. The Belgian medium voltage electricity production mix, as specified in ecoinvent v3.2 (Wernet et al., 2016), was selected for electricity consumption by the treatment system. 
2.2.4.2. Construction materials and process. Infrastructure size details are presented in Appendix C in Supplementary material. Concrete volumes were calculated with an assumed wall thickness of 0.4 (Schaubroeck et al., 2015). Inventory amounts per volume of concrete (Foley et al., 2010) were used to obtain the necessary data to build the life cycle inventory (construction materials and construction processes). Background processes required for the production of construction materials and construction processes were retrieved from ecoinvent v3.2 (Wernet et al., 2016; Appendix A in Supplementary material). A lifetime of 25 years for the infrastructure was considered (Corominas et al., 2013). 
2.2.4.3. Transportation. The ecoinvent process of transport services by lorry and train in Europe were used to account for the environmental impact during the vehicle operation. A summary of the transport routes is presented in Table 3. 
2.2.4.4. Spreading. The raw manure, biological effluent, compost, and synthetic fertilizer were assumed to be spread on cropland by a broadcaster (the ecoinvent process of Fertilising, by broadcaster in Swiss region was used). Only compost and raw manure were considered to be subsequently incorporated in the soil by a rotary cultivator (the ecoinvent process of Tillage, rotary cultivator in Swiss region was used). The calculated application rates for manure products and mineral fertilizer are given in Appendix D, Table S4.1 in Supplementary material. 
2.2.5. Emissions The equations and calculation details of each emission factor are explained in Appendix B in Supplementary material.</list>

2.2.5.1. Emission to the air from raw manure storage. For the no- treatment scenario, the raw piggery manure was collected and stored in a slurry pit under the stable up to 6 months with an average Belgium annual temperature of 11 °C. Manure needs to be stored for an extended period because in Flanders manure and other fertilizers can only be applied from mid-February until the end of August (VLM, 2010). The calculated emission factors (for CH4, N20, NH3 and NOX) during raw manure storage are given in Appendix D,Table S4.2 in Supplementary material.

2.2.5.2. Emission to the air from biological manure treatment. The carbon dioxide (CO2) produced by the oxidation of the methanol added during the biological treatment was accounted for in the impact assessment. The rest of the CO2 produced was considered biogenic (related to the natural carbon cycle) and not accounted for in the impact assessment. The ammonia and nitrous oxide emissions from the biological treatment process were estimated based on the work of Smet et al. (2003) where these emissions were measured in a similar system.

2.2.5.3. Emission to the air from field application. The field applications of raw manure, biological effluent, compost, and synthetic fertilizer release direct emissions to the air. The calculated emission factors are given in Appendix D, Table S4.3 in Supplementary material.

2.2.5.4. Emission to the soil and water from field application. The phosphorus and nitrate leaching to groundwater from field application of raw manure, biological effluent, compost, and synthetic fertilizer are considered in this study. The calculated leaching factors are given in Appendix D, Table S4.4 in Supplementary material.

Heavy metal emission (copper, zinc, arsenic, chromium, cadmium and lead) due to field application of manure-based products and syn- thetic fertilizers were also considered - heavy metal contents pre- sented in Appendix D, Table S4.5 in Supplementary material.

2.2.6. Avoided utilization of synthetic fertilizers The application of raw manure, biological effluent and compost was assumed to replace a commercial fertilizer. Overall the same approach was taken as that of Schaubroeck et al. (2015). Nitrogen (N) and phosphorus (P) contained in the manure-based products were con- sidered to replace N and P from synthetic fertilizers (Appendix B in Supplementary material). The average composition of Belgian synthetic fertilizer mixes (Appendix D, Table S4.6 in Supplementary material) were based on fertilizer consumption statistics from the International Fertilizer Industry Association for different kinds of synthetic fertilizers in Belgium (for single N and P fertilizer mixes) and Western and Central Europe (binary N&P fertilizer mixes) in the year of 2013 (IFA, 2016).

2.2.5.1. Emission to the air from raw manure storage. For the no- treatment scenario, the raw piggery manure was collected and stored in a slurry pit under the stable up to 6 months with an average Belgium annual temperature of 11 °C. Manure needs to be stored for an extended period because in Flanders manure and other fertilizers can only be applied from mid-February until the end of August (VLM, 2010). The calculated emission factors (for CH4, N20, NH3 and NOX) during raw manure storage are given in Appendix D,Table S4.2 in Supplementary material.

2.2.5.2. Emission to the air from biological manure treatment. The carbon dioxide (CO2) produced by the oxidation of the methanol added during the biological treatment was accounted for in the impact assessment. The rest of the CO2 produced was considered biogenic (related to the natural carbon cycle) and not accounted for in the impact assessment. The ammonia and nitrous oxide emissions from the biological treatment process were estimated based on the work of Smet et al. (2003) where these emissions were measured in a similar system.

2.2.5.3. Emission to the air from field application. The field applications of raw manure, biological effluent, compost, and synthetic fertilizer release direct emissions to the air. The calculated emission factors are given in Appendix D, Table S4.3 in Supplementary material.

2.2.5.4. Emission to the soil and water from field application. The phosphorus and nitrate leaching to groundwater from field application of raw manure, biological effluent, compost, and synthetic fertilizer are considered in this study. The calculated leaching factors are given in Appendix D, Table S4.4 in Supplementary material.

Heavy metal emission (copper, zinc, arsenic, chromium, cadmium and lead) due to field application of manure-based products and syn- thetic fertilizers were also considered - heavy metal contents pre- sented in Appendix D, Table S4.5 in Supplementary material.

2.2.6. Avoided utilization of synthetic fertilizers The application of raw manure, biological effluent and compost was assumed to replace a commercial fertilizer. Overall the same approach was taken as that of Schaubroeck et al. (2015). Nitrogen (N) and phosphorus (P) contained in the manure-based products were con- sidered to replace N and P from synthetic fertilizers (Appendix B in Supplementary material). The average composition of Belgian synthetic fertilizer mixes (Appendix D, Table S4.6 in Supplementary material) were based on fertilizer consumption statistics from the International Fertilizer Industry Association for different kinds of synthetic fertilizers in Belgium (for single N and P fertilizer mixes) and Western and Central Europe (binary N&P fertilizer mixes) in the year of 2013 (IFA, 2016).
8: 2.2.3.1. Electricity consumption at the manure treatment plant. A detailed explanation of the electricity consumption calculations is presented in Appendix B in Supplementary material.

The electricity consumption for aeration in the nitrification tank is based on a fine bubble diffused aeration system as described in the biological manure treatment plant by Lemmens et al. (2007). The aeration efficiency is 3-6 kg O2 kWh (Smet et al., 2003). The power estimation for centrifugation was based on the value modelled by De Meester (2013) who is referring to the default power of different types of equipment reported by Perry and Green (1999). Based on that re- ference, a decanter centrifuge with a liquid throughput of 0.0315 r 1 has a typical motor size of 223.81 kW. For mixing of the denitrification reactor, the power estimation refers to the average power consumption per unit volume of the anoxic reactor (Deni- trification tank) of 0.008 kWh.m as described by Tchobanoglous et al. (2003).

An electricity consumption of 16.9 kWh.FU-1 was obtained. Different electricity consumption values can be found in literature. Brockmann et al. (2014) reported 14.5 kWh.FU-1 in their LCA study, Lopez-Ridaura et al. (2009) mentioned 18.7 kWh.FU for the cen- trifuge, aeration and pumping as observed in the work of Levasseur

<list>(2005) and a consumption of around 17 kWh.FU was obtained in a survey in Britanny, France (Béline et al., 2004). Aeration in the biolo- gical reactor, followed by centrifugation, were the main sources of electricity consumption in this and the cited studies. 
2.2.3.2. Electricity consumption for the composting process. The energy use for the large scale tunnel composting installation was assumed to be 45-50 kWh/ton (Lemmens et al., 2007), including pre- and post- treatment, conversion and aeration. 
2.2.4. Background processes 
2.2.4.1. Electricity production. The Belgian medium voltage electricity production mix, as specified in ecoinvent v3.2 (Wernet et al., 2016), was selected for electricity consumption by the treatment system. 
2.2.4.2. Construction materials and process. Infrastructure size details are presented in Appendix C in Supplementary material. Concrete volumes were calculated with an assumed wall thickness of 0.4 (Schaubroeck et al., 2015). Inventory amounts per volume of concrete (Foley et al., 2010) were used to obtain the necessary data to build the life cycle inventory (construction materials and construction processes). Background processes required for the production of construction materials and construction processes were retrieved from ecoinvent v3.2 (Wernet et al., 2016; Appendix A in Supplementary material). A lifetime of 25 years for the infrastructure was considered (Corominas et al., 2013). 
2.2.4.3. Transportation. The ecoinvent process of transport services by lorry and train in Europe were used to account for the environmental impact during the vehicle operation. A summary of the transport routes is presented in Table 3. 
2.2.4.4. Spreading. The raw manure, biological effluent, compost, and synthetic fertilizer were assumed to be spread on cropland by a broadcaster (the ecoinvent process of Fertilising, by broadcaster in Swiss region was used). Only compost and raw manure were considered to be subsequently incorporated in the soil by a rotary cultivator (the ecoinvent process of Tillage, rotary cultivator in Swiss region was used). The calculated application rates for manure products and mineral fertilizer are given in Appendix D, Table S4.1 in Supplementary material. 
2.2.5. Emissions The equations and calculation details of each emission factor are explained in Appendix B in Supplementary material.</list>

Overall the same approach was taken as that of Schaubroeck et al. (2015). Nitrogen (N) and phosphorus (P) contained in the manure-based products were con- sidered to replace N and P from synthetic fertilizers (Appendix B in Supplementary material). The average composition of Belgian synthetic fertilizer mixes (Appendix D, Table S4.6 in Supplementary material) were based on fertilizer consumption statistics from the International Fertilizer Industry Association for different kinds of synthetic fertilizers in Belgium (for single N and P fertilizer mixes) and Western and Central Europe (binary N&P fertilizer mixes) in the year of 2013 (IFA, 2016).
12: 2.2.3.1. Electricity consumption at the manure treatment plant. A detailed explanation of the electricity consumption calculations is presented in Appendix B in Supplementary material.

The electricity consumption for aeration in the nitrification tank is based on a fine bubble diffused aeration system as described in the biological manure treatment plant by Lemmens et al. (2007). The aeration efficiency is 3-6 kg O2 kWh (Smet et al., 2003). The power estimation for centrifugation was based on the value modelled by De Meester (2013) who is referring to the default power of different types of equipment reported by Perry and Green (1999). Based on that re- ference, a decanter centrifuge with a liquid throughput of 0.0315 r 1 has a typical motor size of 223.81 kW. For mixing of the denitrification reactor, the power estimation refers to the average power consumption per unit volume of the anoxic reactor (Deni- trification tank) of 0.008 kWh.m as described by Tchobanoglous et al. (2003).

An electricity consumption of 16.9 kWh.FU-1 was obtained. Different electricity consumption values can be found in literature. Brockmann et al. (2014) reported 14.5 kWh.FU-1 in their LCA study, Lopez-Ridaura et al. (2009) mentioned 18.7 kWh.FU for the cen- trifuge, aeration and pumping as observed in the work of Levasseur

<list>(2005) and a consumption of around 17 kWh.FU was obtained in a survey in Britanny, France (Béline et al., 2004). Aeration in the biolo- gical reactor, followed by centrifugation, were the main sources of electricity consumption in this and the cited studies. 
2.2.3.2. Electricity consumption for the composting process. The energy use for the large scale tunnel composting installation was assumed to be 45-50 kWh/ton (Lemmens et al., 2007), including pre- and post- treatment, conversion and aeration. 
2.2.4. Background processes 
2.2.4.1. Electricity production. The Belgian medium voltage electricity production mix, as specified in ecoinvent v3.2 (Wernet et al., 2016), was selected for electricity consumption by the treatment system. 
2.2.4.2. Construction materials and process. Infrastructure size details are presented in Appendix C in Supplementary material. Concrete volumes were calculated with an assumed wall thickness of 0.4 (Schaubroeck et al., 2015). Inventory amounts per volume of concrete (Foley et al., 2010) were used to obtain the necessary data to build the life cycle inventory (construction materials and construction processes). Background processes required for the production of construction materials and construction processes were retrieved from ecoinvent v3.2 (Wernet et al., 2016; Appendix A in Supplementary material). A lifetime of 25 years for the infrastructure was considered (Corominas et al., 2013). 
2.2.4.3. Transportation. The ecoinvent process of transport services by lorry and train in Europe were used to account for the environmental impact during the vehicle operation. A summary of the transport routes is presented in Table 3. 
2.2.4.4. Spreading. The raw manure, biological effluent, compost, and synthetic fertilizer were assumed to be spread on cropland by a broadcaster (the ecoinvent process of Fertilising, by broadcaster in Swiss region was used). Only compost and raw manure were considered to be subsequently incorporated in the soil by a rotary cultivator (the ecoinvent process of Tillage, rotary cultivator in Swiss region was used). The calculated application rates for manure products and mineral fertilizer are given in Appendix D, Table S4.1 in Supplementary material. 
2.2.5. Emissions The equations and calculation details of each emission factor are explained in Appendix B in Supplementary material.</list>

2.2.5.1. Emission to the air from raw manure storage. For the no- treatment scenario, the raw piggery manure was collected and stored in a slurry pit under the stable up to 6 months with an average Belgium annual temperature of 11 °C. Manure needs to be stored for an extended period because in Flanders manure and other fertilizers can only be applied from mid-February until the end of August (VLM, 2010). The calculated emission factors (for CH4, N20, NH3 and NOX) during raw manure storage are given in Appendix D,Table S4.2 in Supplementary material.

2.2.5.2. Emission to the air from biological manure treatment. The carbon dioxide (CO2) produced by the oxidation of the methanol added during the biological treatment was accounted for in the impact assessment. The rest of the CO2 produced was considered biogenic (related to the natural carbon cycle) and not accounted for in the impact assessment. The ammonia and nitrous oxide emissions from the biological treatment process were estimated based on the work of Smet et al. (2003) where these emissions were measured in a similar system.

2.2.5.3. Emission to the air from field application. The field applications of raw manure, biological effluent, compost, and synthetic fertilizer release direct emissions to the air. The calculated emission factors are given in Appendix D, Table S4.3 in Supplementary material.

2.2.5.4. Emission to the soil and water from field application. The phosphorus and nitrate leaching to groundwater from field application of raw manure, biological effluent, compost, and synthetic fertilizer are considered in this study. The calculated leaching factors are given in Appendix D, Table S4.4 in Supplementary material.

Heavy metal emission (copper, zinc, arsenic, chromium, cadmium and lead) due to field application of manure-based products and syn- thetic fertilizers were also considered - heavy metal contents pre- sented in Appendix D, Table S4.5 in Supplementary material.

2.2.6. Avoided utilization of synthetic fertilizers The application of raw manure, biological effluent and compost was assumed to replace a commercial fertilizer. Overall the same approach was taken as that of Schaubroeck et al. (2015). Nitrogen (N) and phosphorus (P) contained in the manure-based products were con- sidered to replace N and P from synthetic fertilizers (Appendix B in Supplementary material). The average composition of Belgian synthetic fertilizer mixes (Appendix D, Table S4.6 in Supplementary material) were based on fertilizer consumption statistics from the International Fertilizer Industry Association for different kinds of synthetic fertilizers in Belgium (for single N and P fertilizer mixes) and Western and Central Europe (binary N&P fertilizer mixes) in the year of 2013 (IFA, 2016).
13: The Life Cycle Impact Assessment (LCIA) was implemented in SimaPro 8.2 software (PRé Consultants, 2016) and only the mandatory elements of the LCIA according to ISO 14040 (selection of impact ca- tegories, category indicators and characterization models, classifica- tion, and characterization) were utilised. The ReCiPe method version 1.12 was used to quantify the environmental impact at Midpoint (MP) and Endpoint (EP) level in the hierarchic perspective. This method was applied as it is commonly applied for WWTP (Schaubroeck et al., 2015). The results of the LCAs was hence quantified for 18 impact categories.
14: 3.1. Environmental performance of the manure treatment
15: <header>3.1.1. Pinpointing the most relevant impact categories </header>

The life cycle impact assessment (LCIA) results were quantified for 18 ReCiPe midpoint impact categories (Appendix E, Table S5.1 in Supplementary material). The pinpointing process was carried out in two steps. First, the impact categories with the highest influence at endpoint level were selected. Then, some categories were further con- sidered for their importance with respect to the environmental perfor- mance of manure treatment, and hence, impact categories with no available endpoint values were also evaluated. As Flanders is desig- nated as a 100% Nitrate Vulnerable Zone (EC/91/676) the prime goal of manure treatment is to remove excess nutrients such as N and P. Flows with high environmental levels of these macronutrients impact both, freshwater and marine eutrophication, and acidification. The treatment process itself is known for its impact on photochemical oxi- dant formation by emitting NOx and Non-Methane Volatile Organic Compounds (NMVOCs). The aforementioned impact categories at midpoint level are thus included for a fair comparison of treatment versus no treatment. To the contrary, ozone depletion in the strato- sphere is mainly due to chlorofluorocarbons (CFC) and halons, and SO this category was excluded from the following discussion of results since it is considered less relevant for this study.

The main impact categories contributors to endpoint are shown in Fig. 2. Regarding damage to the human health for the treatment/no- treatment scenario (6.13E-05/2.33E-04 DALY), the categories climate change (48%/36% of the endpoint impact), particulate matter forma- tion (26%/56%) and human toxicity (26%/8%) were selected. For damage to ecosystems resulting in diversity loss (2.34E-07/5.52E-07 species.year the most considerable impact categories were climate change (71%/87%), terrestrial ecotoxicity (15%/7%), agricultural land occupation (7%/0.4%) and urban land occupation (4%/2%). Regarding depletion of resource availability (1.02E + 00/6.28E-01 $), fossil

Fig. 3. Relative contribution of the considered processes to the selected impact categories at midpoint level in the treatment and no-treatment scenarios. The major environmental impacts in the treatment scenario (left) originated from electricity production, field application of the effluent and compost and transport of raw manure to the treatment plant.

The selected midpoint impact categories (11 out of 18) that will be discussed further are climate change, particulate matter formation, photochemical oxidant formation, human toxicity, terrestrial ecotoxi- city, freshwater eutrophication, marine eutrophication, terrestrial acidification, agricultural land occupation, urban land occupation, and fossil depletion.

3.1.2. Contribution of the different processes in the treatment scenario 3.1.2.1. Results per category (Fig. 3). Regarding climate change, electricity consumption had the largest impact (25% of the damaging impact; 5.18 kg CO2-eqFU-1 followed by field application of the liquid effluent and the compost, 14 and 15% respectively. The climate change impact from the Belgian medium voltage electricity production mix mainly comes from fossil CO2 released through combustion of natural gas (42%) and hard coal power plants (20%) as mentioned in the ecoinvent database version 3 (Weidema et al., 2013). Considering the climate change impact resulting the field application of the biological effluent, nitrous oxide (N20) emissions contributed to 78% of the damaging impact. Regarding the field application of compost, 50% and 48% of the impact is due to N2O emissions and transportation to Northern France (export), respectively.

Concerning particulate matter (PM) formation potential, this was mainly caused by the field application of compost (56% of the dama- ging impact; 3.44E-02 kg PM10 eq.FU-1), especially through its emis- sion of NH3, which acts as a precursor for particulate formation. On the other hand, the avoided production of synthetic P fertilizer mix (1.01E-02kg PM ¹0 eq.FU-1 due to field application of compost results in 16% mitigation of the particulate matter formation damaging im- pact. The avoided production of synthetic P fertilizer mix gives con- siderable mitigation effects in terms of particulate matter formation since this production results in avoided SOx emissions from the pro- duction of sulphuric acid that will be used for phosphate rock bene- ficiation (Weidema et al., 2013).

Transport of raw manure by lorry from neighbouring farms to the manure treatment plant provides the highest contribution (31%) to the overall photochemical oxidant formation impact due to the NOx emissions during transportation (Weidema et al., 2013). The avoided production of fertilizer mixes mitigates the photochemical oxidant formation impact by 32%, as NO emissions during fertilizer production are avoided.

Heavy metal emissions from field application of effluent and com- post amounted to 48% and 32% of the human toxicity impact, re- spectively. Since synthetic fertilizer contains 15 times more cadmium than the effluent and compost, 32% out of 48% of the prevented impact to human toxicity from the replacement of synthetic fertilizer was due to cadmium emission reduction. Terrestrial ecotoxicity was also mainly affected by heavy metal emissions, particularly copper in the effluent (about 200 times the copper replaced in the synthetic fertilizer).

Freshwater eutrophication caused by the manure treatment process is compensated through the avoided production and application of synthetic fertilizer (89% of the damage is prevented). The main con- tributor of freshwater eutrophication is phosphorus leaching from field application of compost (46%; 2.10E-03 kg P eq.FU-1).

Marine eutrophication is mainly affected by electricity production (58%; 7.79E-02kgN eq.FU-1) for the treatment plant operation. For an electricity production mix in Belgium, nitrate emissions from the used production materials contribute to 85.7% of the marine eu- trophication impact (Weidema et al., 2013).

Terrestrial acidification was mainly induced by NH3 emissions from compost application (70%). The main prevention of the terrestrial acidification damaging impact originates from the avoided synthetic fertilizer mix utilization due to effluent application (19%), as it avoids SO2 emissions produced during the manufacturing process of phosphate fertilizer (Weidema et al., 2013).

Agricultural land occupation mainly originated from electricity consumption in the manure treatment plant (68%; 9.46 E-01 this due to land needed for electricity generation -e.g. in a coal plant land will be needed for mining, the processing and power plants, and disposal area. Urban land occupation mainly origi- nated from the transportation of raw manure, but prevented by the avoided production of synthetic fertilizer.

The environmental impact of fossil fuel depletion primarily origi- nated from the most energy intensive processes, which are electricity, methanol and synthetic P fertilizer production, since these processes need crude oil and natural gas as an energy source (Weidema et al., 2013). Fossil fuel depletion was dominated by 29% through electricity consumption for the treatment plant operations (1.68) kg oil eq.FU-1), 25% through methanol (the carbon source) production (1.41 oil eq.FU-1) and 17% through transportation of raw manure to the treatment plant (9.77E-01 kg oil eq.FU-1). About 35% of the fossil fuel depletion damaging impact is mitigated by the avoided production of
both synthetic N and P fertilizer due to effluent and compost applica- tion (-2.01 oil eq.FU-1.

3.1.2.2. Discussion. Field application of both effluent and compost had a large environmental impact. N2O emissions to the atmosphere during field application largely contributed to the climate change impact, while the emission of NH3 considerably contributed to the particular matter formation and terrestrial acidification. The sensitivity of the climate change impact to N2O and CH4 emissions has previously been described for this type of manure treatment systems (e.g. by ten Hoeve et al., 2014 & Brockmann et al., 2014). Accurate results are thus highly dependent on the uncertainty of the estimation of these emissions. In order to account for this uncertainty, Monte Carlo simulations can be carried out (e.g. De Vries et al., 2015), which increases the complexity of the study but can present a specific range of trust. A full elaborate uncertainty assessment should moreover consider the different types of uncertainty and exclude possible correlation, especially for pairwise comparison between two systems (Nhu et al., 2016; Groen and Heijungs, 2017). As there is no standardized approach that encompasses the complete uncertainty (without correlation), we have not considered this in our study. As an alternative we have pointed out performance differences that are larger than 10% (regarding the LCA impact categories).

The value of the potential climate change impact from the treatment scenario in this study is lower than the values reported in previous LCA studies (Lopez-Ridaura et al., 2009; De Vries et al., 2012; Brockmann et al., 2014). The difference is mainly explained by the fact that, in this study, no emissions (e.g. NH3, CH4) from either the raw manure storage prior biological treatment, nor emissions from the sedimentation tank after the treatment were considered. This is further discussed in Section 3.6 (Future research). Another reason is the difference in N2O emissions from field application. In the study of Brockmann et al. (2014), N2O emissions also originate from ammonium, which in their study is still largely present as 30% of the total nitrogen in the supernatant or the effluent from a biological treatment. In contrast, the amount of am- monium in the effluent of the studied manure treatment is relatively low (4% of the total nitrogen) due to the efficient nitrification process, resulting in lower N2O emissions originating from ammonium during field application.

Electricity consumption contributes considerably to climate change, marine eutrophication, agricultural land occupation and fossil fuel de- pletion. That is due to CO2 emissions, NO3 emissions, the occupation of agricultural land, and also petroleum and natural gas use as the energy source during the electricity production (Weidema et al., 2013). Consequently, the choice of the electricity source has a considerable effect on the resulting environmental performance of the manure treatment process. In this study, a Belgian electricity mix was con- sidered. The contributions of the different power plants for electricity generation in Belgium are 40% nuclear, 21% natural gas, 21% import, 4% solar, 3% coal, 3% wind power, 2% hydro power, and 5% others, as defined in the ecoinvent database version 3.2.

Fig. 3. Relative contribution of the considered processes to the selected impact categories at midpoint level in the treatment and no-treatment scenarios. The major environmental impacts in the treatment scenario (left) originated from electricity production, field application of the effluent and compost and transport of raw manure to the treatment plant.

The selected midpoint impact categories (11 out of 18) that will be discussed further are climate change, particulate matter formation, photochemical oxidant formation, human toxicity, terrestrial ecotoxi- city, freshwater eutrophication, marine eutrophication, terrestrial acidification, agricultural land occupation, urban land occupation, and fossil depletion.

3.1.2. Contribution of the different processes in the treatment scenario 3.1.2.1. Results per category (Fig. 3). Regarding climate change, electricity consumption had the largest impact (25% of the damaging impact; 5.18 kg CO2-eqFU-1 followed by field application of the liquid effluent and the compost, 14 and 15% respectively. The climate change impact from the Belgian medium voltage electricity production mix mainly comes from fossil CO2 released through combustion of natural gas (42%) and hard coal power plants (20%) as mentioned in the ecoinvent database version 3 (Weidema et al., 2013). Considering the climate change impact resulting the field application of the biological effluent, nitrous oxide (N20) emissions contributed to 78% of the damaging impact. Regarding the field application of compost, 50% and 48% of the impact is due to N2O emissions and transportation to Northern France (export), respectively.

Concerning particulate matter (PM) formation potential, this was mainly caused by the field application of compost (56% of the dama- ging impact; 3.44E-02 kg PM10 eq.FU-1), especially through its emis- sion of NH3, which acts as a precursor for particulate formation. On the other hand, the avoided production of synthetic P fertilizer mix (1.01E-02kg PM ¹0 eq.FU-1 due to field application of compost results in 16% mitigation of the particulate matter formation damaging im- pact. The avoided production of synthetic P fertilizer mix gives con- siderable mitigation effects in terms of particulate matter formation since this production results in avoided SOx emissions from the pro- duction of sulphuric acid that will be used for phosphate rock bene- ficiation (Weidema et al., 2013).

Transport of raw manure by lorry from neighbouring farms to the manure treatment plant provides the highest contribution (31%) to the overall photochemical oxidant formation impact due to the NOx emissions during transportation (Weidema et al., 2013). The avoided production of fertilizer mixes mitigates the photochemical oxidant formation impact by 32%, as NO emissions during fertilizer production are avoided.

Heavy metal emissions from field application of effluent and com- post amounted to 48% and 32% of the human toxicity impact, re- spectively. Since synthetic fertilizer contains 15 times more cadmium than the effluent and compost, 32% out of 48% of the prevented impact to human toxicity from the replacement of synthetic fertilizer was due to cadmium emission reduction. Terrestrial ecotoxicity was also mainly affected by heavy metal emissions, particularly copper in the effluent (about 200 times the copper replaced in the synthetic fertilizer).

Freshwater eutrophication caused by the manure treatment process is compensated through the avoided production and application of synthetic fertilizer (89% of the damage is prevented). The main con- tributor of freshwater eutrophication is phosphorus leaching from field application of compost (46%; 2.10E-03 kg P eq.FU-1).

Marine eutrophication is mainly affected by electricity production (58%; 7.79E-02kgN eq.FU-1) for the treatment plant operation. For an electricity production mix in Belgium, nitrate emissions from the used production materials contribute to 85.7% of the marine eu- trophication impact (Weidema et al., 2013).

Terrestrial acidification was mainly induced by NH3 emissions from compost application (70%). The main prevention of the terrestrial acidification damaging impact originates from the avoided synthetic fertilizer mix utilization due to effluent application (19%), as it avoids SO2 emissions produced during the manufacturing process of phosphate fertilizer (Weidema et al., 2013).

Agricultural land occupation mainly originated from electricity consumption in the manure treatment plant (68%; 9.46 E-01 this due to land needed for electricity generation -e.g. in a coal plant land will be needed for mining, the processing and power plants, and disposal area. Urban land occupation mainly origi- nated from the transportation of raw manure, but prevented by the avoided production of synthetic fertilizer.

The environmental impact of fossil fuel depletion primarily origi- nated from the most energy intensive processes, which are electricity, methanol and synthetic P fertilizer production, since these processes need crude oil and natural gas as an energy source (Weidema et al., 2013). Fossil fuel depletion was dominated by 29% through electricity consumption for the treatment plant operations (1.68) kg oil eq.FU-1), 25% through methanol (the carbon source) production (1.41 oil eq.FU-1) and 17% through transportation of raw manure to the treatment plant (9.77E-01 kg oil eq.FU-1). About 35% of the fossil fuel depletion damaging impact is mitigated by the avoided production of
both synthetic N and P fertilizer due to effluent and compost applica- tion (-2.01 oil eq.FU-1.

3.1.2.2. Discussion. Field application of both effluent and compost had a large environmental impact. N2O emissions to the atmosphere during field application largely contributed to the climate change impact, while the emission of NH3 considerably contributed to the particular matter formation and terrestrial acidification. The sensitivity of the climate change impact to N2O and CH4 emissions has previously been described for this type of manure treatment systems (e.g. by ten Hoeve et al., 2014 & Brockmann et al., 2014). Accurate results are thus highly dependent on the uncertainty of the estimation of these emissions. In order to account for this uncertainty, Monte Carlo simulations can be carried out (e.g. De Vries et al., 2015), which increases the complexity of the study but can present a specific range of trust. A full elaborate uncertainty assessment should moreover consider the different types of uncertainty and exclude possible correlation, especially for pairwise comparison between two systems (Nhu et al., 2016; Groen and Heijungs, 2017). As there is no standardized approach that encompasses the complete uncertainty (without correlation), we have not considered this in our study. As an alternative we have pointed out performance differences that are larger than 10% (regarding the LCA impact categories).

The value of the potential climate change impact from the treatment scenario in this study is lower than the values reported in previous LCA studies (Lopez-Ridaura et al., 2009; De Vries et al., 2012; Brockmann et al., 2014). The difference is mainly explained by the fact that, in this study, no emissions (e.g. NH3, CH4) from either the raw manure storage prior biological treatment, nor emissions from the sedimentation tank after the treatment were considered. This is further discussed in Section 3.6 (Future research). Another reason is the difference in N2O emissions from field application. In the study of Brockmann et al. (2014), N2O emissions also originate from ammonium, which in their study is still largely present as 30% of the total nitrogen in the supernatant or the effluent from a biological treatment. In contrast, the amount of am- monium in the effluent of the studied manure treatment is relatively low (4% of the total nitrogen) due to the efficient nitrification process, resulting in lower N2O emissions originating from ammonium during field application.

Electricity consumption contributes considerably to climate change, marine eutrophication, agricultural land occupation and fossil fuel de- pletion. That is due to CO2 emissions, NO3 emissions, the occupation of agricultural land, and also petroleum and natural gas use as the energy source during the electricity production (Weidema et al., 2013). Consequently, the choice of the electricity source has a considerable effect on the resulting environmental performance of the manure treatment process. In this study, a Belgian electricity mix was con- sidered. The contributions of the different power plants for electricity generation in Belgium are 40% nuclear, 21% natural gas, 21% import, 4% solar, 3% coal, 3% wind power, 2% hydro power, and 5% others, as defined in the ecoinvent database version 3.2.
18: <header>3.1.1. Pinpointing the most relevant impact categories </header>

The life cycle impact assessment (LCIA) results were quantified for 18 ReCiPe midpoint impact categories (Appendix E, Table S5.1 in Supplementary material). The pinpointing process was carried out in two steps. First, the impact categories with the highest influence at endpoint level were selected. Then, some categories were further con- sidered for their importance with respect to the environmental perfor- mance of manure treatment, and hence, impact categories with no available endpoint values were also evaluated. As Flanders is desig- nated as a 100% Nitrate Vulnerable Zone (EC/91/676) the prime goal of manure treatment is to remove excess nutrients such as N and P. Flows with high environmental levels of these macronutrients impact both, freshwater and marine eutrophication, and acidification. The treatment process itself is known for its impact on photochemical oxi- dant formation by emitting NOx and Non-Methane Volatile Organic Compounds (NMVOCs). The aforementioned impact categories at midpoint level are thus included for a fair comparison of treatment versus no treatment. To the contrary, ozone depletion in the strato- sphere is mainly due to chlorofluorocarbons (CFC) and halons, and SO this category was excluded from the following discussion of results since it is considered less relevant for this study.

The main impact categories contributors to endpoint are shown in Fig. 2. Regarding damage to the human health for the treatment/no- treatment scenario (6.13E-05/2.33E-04 DALY), the categories climate change (48%/36% of the endpoint impact), particulate matter forma- tion (26%/56%) and human toxicity (26%/8%) were selected. For damage to ecosystems resulting in diversity loss (2.34E-07/5.52E-07 species.year the most considerable impact categories were climate change (71%/87%), terrestrial ecotoxicity (15%/7%), agricultural land occupation (7%/0.4%) and urban land occupation (4%/2%). The climate change impact from the Belgian medium voltage electricity production mix mainly comes from fossil CO2 released through combustion of natural gas (42%) and hard coal power plants (20%) as mentioned in the ecoinvent database version 3 (Weidema et al., 2013). Considering the climate change impact resulting the field application of the biological effluent, nitrous oxide (N20) emissions contributed to 78% of the damaging impact. Regarding the field application of compost, 50% and 48% of the impact is due to N2O emissions and transportation to Northern France (export), respectively.

Concerning particulate matter (PM) formation potential, this was mainly caused by the field application of compost (56% of the dama- ging impact; 3.44E-02 kg PM10 eq.FU-1), especially through its emis- sion of NH3, which acts as a precursor for particulate formation. On the other hand, the avoided production of synthetic P fertilizer mix (1.01E-02kg PM ¹0 eq.FU-1 due to field application of compost results in 16% mitigation of the particulate matter formation damaging im- pact. The avoided production of synthetic P fertilizer mix gives con- siderable mitigation effects in terms of particulate matter formation since this production results in avoided SOx emissions from the pro- duction of sulphuric acid that will be used for phosphate rock bene- ficiation (Weidema et al., 2013).

Transport of raw manure by lorry from neighbouring farms to the manure treatment plant provides the highest contribution (31%) to the overall photochemical oxidant formation impact due to the NOx emissions during transportation (Weidema et al., 2013). The avoided production of fertilizer mixes mitigates the photochemical oxidant formation impact by 32%, as NO emissions during fertilizer production are avoided.

Heavy metal emissions from field application of effluent and com- post amounted to 48% and 32% of the human toxicity impact, re- spectively. Since synthetic fertilizer contains 15 times more cadmium than the effluent and compost, 32% out of 48% of the prevented impact to human toxicity from the replacement of synthetic fertilizer was due to cadmium emission reduction. Terrestrial ecotoxicity was also mainly affected by heavy metal emissions, particularly copper in the effluent (about 200 times the copper replaced in the synthetic fertilizer).

Freshwater eutrophication caused by the manure treatment process is compensated through the avoided production and application of synthetic fertilizer (89% of the damage is prevented). The main con- tributor of freshwater eutrophication is phosphorus leaching from field application of compost (46%; 2.10E-03 kg P eq.FU-1).

Marine eutrophication is mainly affected by electricity production (58%; 7.79E-02kgN eq.FU-1) for the treatment plant operation. For an electricity production mix in Belgium, nitrate emissions from the used production materials contribute to 85.7% of the marine eu- trophication impact (Weidema et al., 2013).

Terrestrial acidification was mainly induced by NH3 emissions from compost application (70%). The main prevention of the terrestrial acidification damaging impact originates from the avoided synthetic fertilizer mix utilization due to effluent application (19%), as it avoids SO2 emissions produced during the manufacturing process of phosphate fertilizer (Weidema et al., 2013).

Agricultural land occupation mainly originated from electricity consumption in the manure treatment plant (68%; 9.46 E-01 this due to land needed for electricity generation -e.g. in a coal plant land will be needed for mining, the processing and power plants, and disposal area. Urban land occupation mainly origi- nated from the transportation of raw manure, but prevented by the avoided production of synthetic fertilizer.

The environmental impact of fossil fuel depletion primarily origi- nated from the most energy intensive processes, which are electricity, methanol and synthetic P fertilizer production, since these processes need crude oil and natural gas as an energy source (Weidema et al., 2013). Fossil fuel depletion was dominated by 29% through electricity consumption for the treatment plant operations (1.68) kg oil eq.FU-1), 25% through methanol (the carbon source) production (1.41 oil eq.FU-1) and 17% through transportation of raw manure to the treatment plant (9.77E-01 kg oil eq.FU-1). About 35% of the fossil fuel depletion damaging impact is mitigated by the avoided production of
both synthetic N and P fertilizer due to effluent and compost applica- tion (-2.01 oil eq.FU-1.

3.1.2.2. Discussion. Field application of both effluent and compost had a large environmental impact. N2O emissions to the atmosphere during field application largely contributed to the climate change impact, while the emission of NH3 considerably contributed to the particular matter formation and terrestrial acidification. The sensitivity of the climate change impact to N2O and CH4 emissions has previously been described for this type of manure treatment systems (e.g. by ten Hoeve et al., 2014 & Brockmann et al., 2014). Accurate results are thus highly dependent on the uncertainty of the estimation of these emissions. In order to account for this uncertainty, Monte Carlo simulations can be carried out (e.g. De Vries et al., 2015), which increases the complexity of the study but can present a specific range of trust. A full elaborate uncertainty assessment should moreover consider the different types of uncertainty and exclude possible correlation, especially for pairwise comparison between two systems (Nhu et al., 2016; Groen and Heijungs, 2017). As there is no standardized approach that encompasses the complete uncertainty (without correlation), we have not considered this in our study. As an alternative we have pointed out performance differences that are larger than 10% (regarding the LCA impact categories).

The value of the potential climate change impact from the treatment scenario in this study is lower than the values reported in previous LCA studies (Lopez-Ridaura et al., 2009; De Vries et al., 2012; Brockmann et al., 2014). The difference is mainly explained by the fact that, in this study, no emissions (e.g. NH3, CH4) from either the raw manure storage prior biological treatment, nor emissions from the sedimentation tank after the treatment were considered. This is further discussed in Section 3.6 (Future research). Another reason is the difference in N2O emissions from field application. In the study of Brockmann et al. (2014), N2O emissions also originate from ammonium, which in their study is still largely present as 30% of the total nitrogen in the supernatant or the effluent from a biological treatment. In contrast, the amount of am- monium in the effluent of the studied manure treatment is relatively low (4% of the total nitrogen) due to the efficient nitrification process, resulting in lower N2O emissions originating from ammonium during field application.

Electricity consumption contributes considerably to climate change, marine eutrophication, agricultural land occupation and fossil fuel de- pletion. That is due to CO2 emissions, NO3 emissions, the occupation of agricultural land, and also petroleum and natural gas use as the energy source during the electricity production (Weidema et al., 2013). Consequently, the choice of the electricity source has a considerable effect on the resulting environmental performance of the manure treatment process. In this study, a Belgian electricity mix was con- sidered. The contributions of the different power plants for electricity generation in Belgium are 40% nuclear, 21% natural gas, 21% import, 4% solar, 3% coal, 3% wind power, 2% hydro power, and 5% others, as defined in the ecoinvent database version 3.2.
21: <header>3.1.1. Pinpointing the most relevant impact categories </header>

The life cycle impact assessment (LCIA) results were quantified for 18 ReCiPe midpoint impact categories (Appendix E, Table S5.1 in Supplementary material). The pinpointing process was carried out in two steps. First, the impact categories with the highest influence at endpoint level were selected. Then, some categories were further con- sidered for their importance with respect to the environmental perfor- mance of manure treatment, and hence, impact categories with no available endpoint values were also evaluated. As Flanders is desig- nated as a 100% Nitrate Vulnerable Zone (EC/91/676) the prime goal of manure treatment is to remove excess nutrients such as N and P. Flows with high environmental levels of these macronutrients impact both, freshwater and marine eutrophication, and acidification. The treatment process itself is known for its impact on photochemical oxi- dant formation by emitting NOx and Non-Methane Volatile Organic Compounds (NMVOCs). The aforementioned impact categories at midpoint level are thus included for a fair comparison of treatment versus no treatment. To the contrary, ozone depletion in the strato- sphere is mainly due to chlorofluorocarbons (CFC) and halons, and SO this category was excluded from the following discussion of results since it is considered less relevant for this study.

The main impact categories contributors to endpoint are shown in Fig. 2. Regarding damage to the human health for the treatment/no- treatment scenario (6.13E-05/2.33E-04 DALY), the categories climate change (48%/36% of the endpoint impact), particulate matter forma- tion (26%/56%) and human toxicity (26%/8%) were selected. For damage to ecosystems resulting in diversity loss (2.34E-07/5.52E-07 species.year the most considerable impact categories were climate change (71%/87%), terrestrial ecotoxicity (15%/7%), agricultural land occupation (7%/0.4%) and urban land occupation (4%/2%). Regarding depletion of resource availability (1.02E + 00/6.28E-01 $), fossil

The major environmental impacts in the treatment scenario (left) originated from electricity production, field application of the effluent and compost and transport of raw manure to the treatment plant.

On the other hand, the avoided production of synthetic P fertilizer mix (1.01E-02kg PM ¹0 eq.FU-1 due to field application of compost results in 16% mitigation of the particulate matter formation damaging im- pact. The avoided production of synthetic P fertilizer mix gives con- siderable mitigation effects in terms of particulate matter formation since this production results in avoided SOx emissions from the pro- duction of sulphuric acid that will be used for phosphate rock bene- ficiation (Weidema et al., 2013).

Transport of raw manure by lorry from neighbouring farms to the manure treatment plant provides the highest contribution (31%) to the overall photochemical oxidant formation impact due to the NOx emissions during transportation (Weidema et al., 2013). The avoided production of fertilizer mixes mitigates the photochemical oxidant formation impact by 32%, as NO emissions during fertilizer production are avoided.

Heavy metal emissions from field application of effluent and com- post amounted to 48% and 32% of the human toxicity impact, re- spectively. Since synthetic fertilizer contains 15 times more cadmium than the effluent and compost, 32% out of 48% of the prevented impact to human toxicity from the replacement of synthetic fertilizer was due to cadmium emission reduction. Terrestrial ecotoxicity was also mainly affected by heavy metal emissions, particularly copper in the effluent (about 200 times the copper replaced in the synthetic fertilizer).

Freshwater eutrophication caused by the manure treatment process is compensated through the avoided production and application of synthetic fertilizer (89% of the damage is prevented). The main con- tributor of freshwater eutrophication is phosphorus leaching from field application of compost (46%; 2.10E-03 kg P eq.FU-1).

Marine eutrophication is mainly affected by electricity production (58%; 7.79E-02kgN eq.FU-1) for the treatment plant operation. For an electricity production mix in Belgium, nitrate emissions from the used production materials contribute to 85.7% of the marine eu- trophication impact (Weidema et al., 2013).

Terrestrial acidification was mainly induced by NH3 emissions from compost application (70%). The main prevention of the terrestrial acidification damaging impact originates from the avoided synthetic fertilizer mix utilization due to effluent application (19%), as it avoids SO2 emissions produced during the manufacturing process of phosphate fertilizer (Weidema et al., 2013).

Agricultural land occupation mainly originated from electricity consumption in the manure treatment plant (68%; 9.46 E-01 this due to land needed for electricity generation -e.g. in a coal plant land will be needed for mining, the processing and power plants, and disposal area. Urban land occupation mainly origi- nated from the transportation of raw manure, but prevented by the avoided production of synthetic fertilizer.

The environmental impact of fossil fuel depletion primarily origi- nated from the most energy intensive processes, which are electricity, methanol and synthetic P fertilizer production, since these processes need crude oil and natural gas as an energy source (Weidema et al., 2013). Fossil fuel depletion was dominated by 29% through electricity consumption for the treatment plant operations (1.68) kg oil eq.FU-1), 25% through methanol (the carbon source) production (1.41 oil eq.FU-1) and 17% through transportation of raw manure to the treatment plant (9.77E-01 kg oil eq.FU-1). About 35% of the fossil fuel depletion damaging impact is mitigated by the avoided production of
both synthetic N and P fertilizer due to effluent and compost applica- tion (-2.01 oil eq.FU-1.

3.1.2.2. Discussion. Field application of both effluent and compost had a large environmental impact. N2O emissions to the atmosphere during field application largely contributed to the climate change impact, while the emission of NH3 considerably contributed to the particular matter formation and terrestrial acidification. The sensitivity of the climate change impact to N2O and CH4 emissions has previously been described for this type of manure treatment systems (e.g. by ten Hoeve et al., 2014 & Brockmann et al., 2014). Accurate results are thus highly dependent on the uncertainty of the estimation of these emissions. In order to account for this uncertainty, Monte Carlo simulations can be carried out (e.g. De Vries et al., 2015), which increases the complexity of the study but can present a specific range of trust. A full elaborate uncertainty assessment should moreover consider the different types of uncertainty and exclude possible correlation, especially for pairwise comparison between two systems (Nhu et al., 2016; Groen and Heijungs, 2017). As there is no standardized approach that encompasses the complete uncertainty (without correlation), we have not considered this in our study. As an alternative we have pointed out performance differences that are larger than 10% (regarding the LCA impact categories).

The value of the potential climate change impact from the treatment scenario in this study is lower than the values reported in previous LCA studies (Lopez-Ridaura et al., 2009; De Vries et al., 2012; Brockmann et al., 2014). The difference is mainly explained by the fact that, in this study, no emissions (e.g. NH3, CH4) from either the raw manure storage prior biological treatment, nor emissions from the sedimentation tank after the treatment were considered. This is further discussed in Section 3.6 (Future research). Another reason is the difference in N2O emissions from field application. In the study of Brockmann et al. (2014), N2O emissions also originate from ammonium, which in their study is still largely present as 30% of the total nitrogen in the supernatant or the effluent from a biological treatment. In contrast, the amount of am- monium in the effluent of the studied manure treatment is relatively low (4% of the total nitrogen) due to the efficient nitrification process, resulting in lower N2O emissions originating from ammonium during field application.

Electricity consumption contributes considerably to climate change, marine eutrophication, agricultural land occupation and fossil fuel de- pletion. That is due to CO2 emissions, NO3 emissions, the occupation of agricultural land, and also petroleum and natural gas use as the energy source during the electricity production (Weidema et al., 2013). Consequently, the choice of the electricity source has a considerable effect on the resulting environmental performance of the manure treatment process. In this study, a Belgian electricity mix was con- sidered. The contributions of the different power plants for electricity generation in Belgium are 40% nuclear, 21% natural gas, 21% import, 4% solar, 3% coal, 3% wind power, 2% hydro power, and 5% others, as defined in the ecoinvent database version 3.2.
24: <header>3.1.1. Pinpointing the most relevant impact categories </header>

The life cycle impact assessment (LCIA) results were quantified for 18 ReCiPe midpoint impact categories (Appendix E, Table S5.1 in Supplementary material). The pinpointing process was carried out in two steps. First, the impact categories with the highest influence at endpoint level were selected. Then, some categories were further con- sidered for their importance with respect to the environmental perfor- mance of manure treatment, and hence, impact categories with no available endpoint values were also evaluated. As Flanders is desig- nated as a 100% Nitrate Vulnerable Zone (EC/91/676) the prime goal of manure treatment is to remove excess nutrients such as N and P. Flows with high environmental levels of these macronutrients impact both, freshwater and marine eutrophication, and acidification. The treatment process itself is known for its impact on photochemical oxi- dant formation by emitting NOx and Non-Methane Volatile Organic Compounds (NMVOCs). The aforementioned impact categories at midpoint level are thus included for a fair comparison of treatment versus no treatment. To the contrary, ozone depletion in the strato- sphere is mainly due to chlorofluorocarbons (CFC) and halons, and SO this category was excluded from the following discussion of results since it is considered less relevant for this study.

The main impact categories contributors to endpoint are shown in Fig. 2. Regarding damage to the human health for the treatment/no- treatment scenario (6.13E-05/2.33E-04 DALY), the categories climate change (48%/36% of the endpoint impact), particulate matter forma- tion (26%/56%) and human toxicity (26%/8%) were selected. For damage to ecosystems resulting in diversity loss (2.34E-07/5.52E-07 species.year the most considerable impact categories were climate change (71%/87%), terrestrial ecotoxicity (15%/7%), agricultural land occupation (7%/0.4%) and urban land occupation (4%/2%).
