Heavy metal emissions from field application of effluent and com- post amounted to 48% and 32% of the human toxicity impact, re- spectively. Since synthetic fertilizer contains 15 times more cadmium than the effluent and compost, 32% out of 48% of the prevented impact to human toxicity from the replacement of synthetic fertilizer was due to cadmium emission reduction. Terrestrial ecotoxicity was also mainly affected by heavy metal emissions, particularly copper in the effluent (about 200 times the copper replaced in the synthetic fertilizer).

Freshwater eutrophication caused by the manure treatment process is compensated through the avoided production and application of synthetic fertilizer (89% of the damage is prevented). The main con- tributor of freshwater eutrophication is phosphorus leaching from field application of compost (46%; 2.10E-03 kg P eq.FU-1).

Marine eutrophication is mainly affected by electricity production (58%; 7.79E-02kgN eq.FU-1) for the treatment plant operation. For an electricity production mix in Belgium, nitrate emissions from the used production materials contribute to 85.7% of the marine eu- trophication impact (Weidema et al., 2013).

Terrestrial acidification was mainly induced by NH3 emissions from compost application (70%). The main prevention of the terrestrial acidification damaging impact originates from the avoided synthetic fertilizer mix utilization due to effluent application (19%), as it avoids SO2 emissions produced during the manufacturing process of phosphate fertilizer (Weidema et al., 2013).

Agricultural land occupation mainly originated from electricity consumption in the manure treatment plant (68%; 9.46 E-01 this due to land needed for electricity generation -e.g. in a coal plant land will be needed for mining, the processing and power plants, and disposal area. Urban land occupation mainly origi- nated from the transportation of raw manure, but prevented by the avoided production of synthetic fertilizer.

The environmental impact of fossil fuel depletion primarily origi- nated from the most energy intensive processes, which are electricity, methanol and synthetic P fertilizer production, since these processes need crude oil and natural gas as an energy source (Weidema et al., 2013). Fossil fuel depletion was dominated by 29% through electricity consumption for the treatment plant operations (1.68) kg oil eq.FU-1), 25% through methanol (the carbon source) production (1.41 oil eq.FU-1) and 17% through transportation of raw manure to the treatment plant (9.77E-01 kg oil eq.FU-1). About 35% of the fossil fuel depletion damaging impact is mitigated by the avoided production of
both synthetic N and P fertilizer due to effluent and compost applica- tion (-2.01 oil eq.FU-1.

3.1.2.2. Discussion. Field application of both effluent and compost had a large environmental impact. N2O emissions to the atmosphere during field application largely contributed to the climate change impact, while the emission of NH3 considerably contributed to the particular matter formation and terrestrial acidification. The sensitivity of the climate change impact to N2O and CH4 emissions has previously been described for this type of manure treatment systems (e.g. by ten Hoeve et al., 2014 & Brockmann et al., 2014). Accurate results are thus highly dependent on the uncertainty of the estimation of these emissions. In order to account for this uncertainty, Monte Carlo simulations can be carried out (e.g. De Vries et al., 2015), which increases the complexity of the study but can present a specific range of trust. A full elaborate uncertainty assessment should moreover consider the different types of uncertainty and exclude possible correlation, especially for pairwise comparison between two systems (Nhu et al., 2016; Groen and Heijungs, 2017). As there is no standardized approach that encompasses the complete uncertainty (without correlation), we have not considered this in our study. As an alternative we have pointed out performance differences that are larger than 10% (regarding the LCA impact categories).

The value of the potential climate change impact from the treatment scenario in this study is lower than the values reported in previous LCA studies (Lopez-Ridaura et al., 2009; De Vries et al., 2012; Brockmann et al., 2014). The difference is mainly explained by the fact that, in this study, no emissions (e.g. NH3, CH4) from either the raw manure storage prior biological treatment, nor emissions from the sedimentation tank after the treatment were considered. This is further discussed in Section 3.6 (Future research). Another reason is the difference in N2O emissions from field application. In the study of Brockmann et al. (2014), N2O emissions also originate from ammonium, which in their study is still largely present as 30% of the total nitrogen in the supernatant or the effluent from a biological treatment. In contrast, the amount of am- monium in the effluent of the studied manure treatment is relatively low (4% of the total nitrogen) due to the efficient nitrification process, resulting in lower N2O emissions originating from ammonium during field application.

Electricity consumption contributes considerably to climate change, marine eutrophication, agricultural land occupation and fossil fuel de- pletion. That is due to CO2 emissions, NO3 emissions, the occupation of agricultural land, and also petroleum and natural gas use as the energy source during the electricity production (Weidema et al., 2013). Consequently, the choice of the electricity source has a considerable effect on the resulting environmental performance of the manure treatment process. In this study, a Belgian electricity mix was con- sidered. The contributions of the different power plants for electricity generation in Belgium are 40% nuclear, 21% natural gas, 21% import, 4% solar, 3% coal, 3% wind power, 2% hydro power, and 5% others, as defined in the ecoinvent database version 3.2.
27: The net climate change impact of the treatment scenario is 46% lower than the no-treatment scenario. Although higher synthetic ferti- lizer amounts are displaced by the application of raw manure, this benefit could not compensate the damaging impact caused by the N2O emissions from field application of raw manure and the CH4 emission from raw manure storage. This is in accordance with Brockmann et al. (2014) and Lopez-Ridaura et al. (2009), who found that besides the emission from field application, the emissions during long-period sto- rage of raw manure in the no-treatment scenario dominate the climate change impact.
The net impact of potential photochemical oxidation of the

treatment scenario is 131% higher than that of the no-treatment. This is a result of the higher amounts of avoided NO emission from N&P synthetic fertilizer mix production in the no-treatment scenario. However, without the compensation for avoided synthetic fertilizer, the damage caused by the treatment scenario (6.79E-02kg NMVOC) is lower than the damage caused by the no-treatment one (1.21E-01 kg NMVOC). In both cases, the damaging impact is mainly induced by NO emissions during the transportation process, either of raw manure from the farm to the treatment plant or of raw manure to the agriculture field where it can be spread, a distance three times longer than in the treatment scenario.

The net particulate matter formation of the treatment scenario was one order of magnitude lower than the one in the no-treatment sce- nario. Increased NH3 emissions during field application of raw manure were the main source of this difference. The same trend was observed in the work of Brockmann et al. (2014). As the goal of the raw manure treatment is to reduce its nitrogen content by initrification-denitrifica- tion, NH3 emissions from field application of the treated manure pro- ducts (effluent and compost) are lower than from untreated raw manure. Even with a higher mitigation from the substitution of syn- thetic fertilizer utilisation in the no-treatment scenario, only 11% of the particulate matter formation from NH3 emissions during storage and field application of raw manure were mitigated by this substitution.

The damaging impacts caused by the treatment scenario to human toxicity and terrestrial ecotoxicity (2.28E + 01 kg 1.4-DB eq. and 2.26E-01 1.4-DB eq., respectively) are similar to those of the no- treatment scenario (2.55E + 01 kg 1.4-DB eq. and 2.75E-01 kg 1.4-DB eq., respectively) as in fact the biological treatment and composting processes do not remove heavy metals, which are responsible of 80%/ 98% human toxicity/terrestrial ecotoxicity damaging impact values. However, since effluent and compost have lower nitrogen contents than raw manure, the amount of avoided synthetic fertilizer utilisation and associated heavy metal emission in the no-treatment scenario is higher.

The net impact of freshwater eutrophication (only dealing with P- compounds) shows a negative value (damage mitigation) for the no- treatment and a positive value (damage) for the treatment scenario. However, the damage in the treatment scenario (4.52E-03 kg P) is lower than the damage caused in the no-treatment scenario (5.41E-03 kg P). In the treatment scenario, a large share of P separated into the solid fraction eventually becomes compost. This nutrient in the compost has been converted into a much more stable form (Bernal et al., 2009) and consequently compost has a lower phosphorus leaching potential in comparison to raw manure. But it also has a lower work efficiency compared to P in manure products. This explains the difference in net damage between the two scenarios, as a lower amount of synthetic P fertilizer mix can be substituted in the treatment scenario.

The treatment scenario shows a 53% lower net potential marine eutrophication impact than the no-treatment. This in spite of the fact that only 22% of the marine eutrophication damaging impact in the treatment scenario is counteracted by the substitution of synthetic fertilizers, in comparison to 34% of the damage prevented in the no- treatment scenario. However, the damaging impact in the no-treatment scenario (3.41E-01 kg eq.), which originates mainly from nitrate leaching and ammonia emissions during the field application of manure, more than doubles the damaging impact from the treatment scenario (1.35E-01 N eq.), which was mainly induced by electricity consumption. Similarly, Brockmann et al. (2014) reported that the total marine eutrophication potential from the no-treatment is twice as high as that of the treatment scenario. ten Hoeve et al. (2014) reported that the manure treatment with centrifugation gave a 31% lower net marine eutrophication impact. To the contrary, Lopez-Ridaura et al. (2009) found a higher eutrophication potential in the treatment scenario be- cause of the high NH3 emission during the biological treatment, whilst the biological treatment in our study is considered to have low NH3 emissions (Smet et al., 2003). Thus, the treatment scenario scores better in terms of marine eutrophication, as the net damage caused is less than
half of that in the no-treatment scenario.

The net impact on terrestrial acidification was 95% lower for the treatment scenario than for the no-treatment one. The emission of NH3 from the field application is the most considerable contributor to this category. In the treatment scenario, NH3 emission is very small because: (1) the NH3 emission from the raw manure storage in the treatment plant is assumed to be zero as it occurs in a closed system; (2) the NH3 emission during biological treatment is very low (2% of the total im- pact) due to optimum nitrification conditions in the aerobic reactor (Smet et al., 2003); (3) the composting process occurs in a closed tunnel, hence, the gaseous emissions during the composting process were deemed to be zero (Lemmens et al., 2007); and (4) most of the nitrogen content ends up in the liquid fraction after centrifugation, which is then followed by the biological treatment with efficient ni- trogen removal. Likewise, Brockmann et al. (2014) and Lopez-Ridaura et al. (2009) found that manure treatment has a better environmental performance than no-treatment in terms of potential terrestrial acid- ification, even though the difference between the two scenarios is not SO large since emission from slurry storage is included, and the emission from their biological treatment is higher.

The treatment scenario caused a higher agricultural land occupation especially due to the higher energy consumption through electricity for the treatment plant operations and the composting process. In the no- treatment scenario, the only processes that consume energy are the transportation to and the spreading of raw manure on the cropland, two processes with a low impact on agricultural land occupation.

For urban land occupation, the damaging impact caused by the treatment scenario is 32% lower than the no-treatment scenario mainly because of the transportation processes involved. Even though both scenarios have similar loads, the distance travelled in the no-treatment scenario is more than three times that in the treatment scenario. While both scenarios result in actual urban land occupation impact remedia- tion, the net remediation in the no-treatment scenario is more than two times larger than the one of the treatment scenario. This mainly thanks to the higher fertilizer substitution in the no-treatment scenario.

The treatment scenario poses higher fossil fuel depletion damaging impact than the no-treatment scenario (41% higher). In the treatment scenario, the energy demand is primarily needed for the provision of electricity, chemical products and transportation. The fossil fuel de- pletion impact from the Belgian medium voltage electricity production mix, required to support the biological treatment and the composting processes, is mainly due to the utilisation of natural gas (50%) and coal (16%) as energy sources (Weidema et al., 2013). In contrast, the fossil fuel consumption of the no-treatment scenario is only associated with field operations such as transportation and spreading of the raw manure. Because of the higher nitrogen content in the raw manure, a higher amount of synthetic fertilizers could additionally be substituted. Hence, the net effect of the no-treatment scenario is a potential fossil fuel saving. This finding is also reported by ten Hoeve et al. (2014); Brockmann et al. (2014); De Vries et al. (2012); and Lopez-Ridaura et al. (2009).

(2009) found a higher eutrophication potential in the treatment scenario be- cause of the high NH3 emission during the biological treatment, whilst the biological treatment in our study is considered to have low NH3 emissions (Smet et al., 2003). Thus, the treatment scenario scores better in terms of marine eutrophication, as the net damage caused is less than
half of that in the no-treatment scenario.

The net impact on terrestrial acidification was 95% lower for the treatment scenario than for the no-treatment one. The emission of NH3 from the field application is the most considerable contributor to this category. In the treatment scenario, NH3 emission is very small because: (1) the NH3 emission from the raw manure storage in the treatment plant is assumed to be zero as it occurs in a closed system; (2) the NH3 emission during biological treatment is very low (2% of the total im- pact) due to optimum nitrification conditions in the aerobic reactor (Smet et al., 2003); (3) the composting process occurs in a closed tunnel, hence, the gaseous emissions during the composting process were deemed to be zero (Lemmens et al., 2007); and (4) most of the nitrogen content ends up in the liquid fraction after centrifugation, which is then followed by the biological treatment with efficient ni- trogen removal. Likewise, Brockmann et al. (2014) and Lopez-Ridaura et al. (2009) found that manure treatment has a better environmental performance than no-treatment in terms of potential terrestrial acid- ification, even though the difference between the two scenarios is not SO large since emission from slurry storage is included, and the emission from their biological treatment is higher.

The treatment scenario caused a higher agricultural land occupation especially due to the higher energy consumption through electricity for the treatment plant operations and the composting process. In the no- treatment scenario, the only processes that consume energy are the transportation to and the spreading of raw manure on the cropland, two processes with a low impact on agricultural land occupation.

For urban land occupation, the damaging impact caused by the treatment scenario is 32% lower than the no-treatment scenario mainly because of the transportation processes involved. Even though both scenarios have similar loads, the distance travelled in the no-treatment scenario is more than three times that in the treatment scenario. While both scenarios result in actual urban land occupation impact remedia- tion, the net remediation in the no-treatment scenario is more than two times larger than the one of the treatment scenario. This mainly thanks to the higher fertilizer substitution in the no-treatment scenario.

The treatment scenario poses higher fossil fuel depletion damaging impact than the no-treatment scenario (41% higher). In the treatment scenario, the energy demand is primarily needed for the provision of electricity, chemical products and transportation. The fossil fuel de- pletion impact from the Belgian medium voltage electricity production mix, required to support the biological treatment and the composting processes, is mainly due to the utilisation of natural gas (50%) and coal (16%) as energy sources (Weidema et al., 2013). In contrast, the fossil fuel consumption of the no-treatment scenario is only associated with field operations such as transportation and spreading of the raw manure. Because of the higher nitrogen content in the raw manure, a higher amount of synthetic fertilizers could additionally be substituted. Hence, the net effect of the no-treatment scenario is a potential fossil fuel saving. This finding is also reported by ten Hoeve et al. (2014); Brockmann et al. (2014); De Vries et al. (2012); and Lopez-Ridaura et al. (2009).
31: The net climate change impact of the treatment scenario is 46% lower than the no-treatment scenario. Although higher synthetic ferti- lizer amounts are displaced by the application of raw manure, this benefit could not compensate the damaging impact caused by the N2O emissions from field application of raw manure and the CH4 emission from raw manure storage. This is in accordance with Brockmann et al. (2014) and Lopez-Ridaura et al. (2009), who found that besides the emission from field application, the emissions during long-period sto- rage of raw manure in the no-treatment scenario dominate the climate change impact.
The net impact of potential photochemical oxidation of the

treatment scenario is 131% higher than that of the no-treatment. This is a result of the higher amounts of avoided NO emission from N&P synthetic fertilizer mix production in the no-treatment scenario. However, without the compensation for avoided synthetic fertilizer, the damage caused by the treatment scenario (6.79E-02kg NMVOC) is lower than the damage caused by the no-treatment one (1.21E-01 kg NMVOC). In both cases, the damaging impact is mainly induced by NO emissions during the transportation process, either of raw manure from the farm to the treatment plant or of raw manure to the agriculture field where it can be spread, a distance three times longer than in the treatment scenario.

The net particulate matter formation of the treatment scenario was one order of magnitude lower than the one in the no-treatment sce- nario. Increased NH3 emissions during field application of raw manure were the main source of this difference. The same trend was observed in the work of Brockmann et al. (2014). As the goal of the raw manure treatment is to reduce its nitrogen content by initrification-denitrifica- tion, NH3 emissions from field application of the treated manure pro- ducts (effluent and compost) are lower than from untreated raw manure. Even with a higher mitigation from the substitution of syn- thetic fertilizer utilisation in the no-treatment scenario, only 11% of the particulate matter formation from NH3 emissions during storage and field application of raw manure were mitigated by this substitution.

The damaging impacts caused by the treatment scenario to human toxicity and terrestrial ecotoxicity (2.28E + 01 kg 1.4-DB eq. and 2.26E-01 1.4-DB eq., respectively) are similar to those of the no- treatment scenario (2.55E + 01 kg 1.4-DB eq. and 2.75E-01 kg 1.4-DB eq., respectively) as in fact the biological treatment and composting processes do not remove heavy metals, which are responsible of 80%/ 98% human toxicity/terrestrial ecotoxicity damaging impact values. However, since effluent and compost have lower nitrogen contents than raw manure, the amount of avoided synthetic fertilizer utilisation and associated heavy metal emission in the no-treatment scenario is higher.

The net impact of freshwater eutrophication (only dealing with P- compounds) shows a negative value (damage mitigation) for the no- treatment and a positive value (damage) for the treatment scenario. However, the damage in the treatment scenario (4.52E-03 kg P) is lower than the damage caused in the no-treatment scenario (5.41E-03 kg P). In the treatment scenario, a large share of P separated into the solid fraction eventually becomes compost. This nutrient in the compost has been converted into a much more stable form (Bernal et al., 2009) and consequently compost has a lower phosphorus leaching potential in comparison to raw manure. But it also has a lower work efficiency compared to P in manure products. This explains the difference in net damage between the two scenarios, as a lower amount of synthetic P fertilizer mix can be substituted in the treatment scenario.

The treatment scenario shows a 53% lower net potential marine eutrophication impact than the no-treatment. This in spite of the fact that only 22% of the marine eutrophication damaging impact in the treatment scenario is counteracted by the substitution of synthetic fertilizers, in comparison to 34% of the damage prevented in the no- treatment scenario. However, the damaging impact in the no-treatment scenario (3.41E-01 kg eq.), which originates mainly from nitrate leaching and ammonia emissions during the field application of manure, more than doubles the damaging impact from the treatment scenario (1.35E-01 N eq.), which was mainly induced by electricity consumption. Similarly, Brockmann et al. (2014) reported that the total marine eutrophication potential from the no-treatment is twice as high as that of the treatment scenario. ten Hoeve et al. (2014) reported that the manure treatment with centrifugation gave a 31% lower net marine eutrophication impact. To the contrary, Lopez-Ridaura et al. (2009) found a higher eutrophication potential in the treatment scenario be- cause of the high NH3 emission during the biological treatment, whilst the biological treatment in our study is considered to have low NH3 emissions (Smet et al., 2003). half of that in the no-treatment scenario.

While both scenarios result in actual urban land occupation impact remedia- tion, the net remediation in the no-treatment scenario is more than two times larger than the one of the treatment scenario. This mainly thanks to the higher fertilizer substitution in the no-treatment scenario.

The treatment scenario poses higher fossil fuel depletion damaging impact than the no-treatment scenario (41% higher). In the treatment scenario, the energy demand is primarily needed for the provision of electricity, chemical products and transportation. The fossil fuel de- pletion impact from the Belgian medium voltage electricity production mix, required to support the biological treatment and the composting processes, is mainly due to the utilisation of natural gas (50%) and coal (16%) as energy sources (Weidema et al., 2013). In contrast, the fossil fuel consumption of the no-treatment scenario is only associated with field operations such as transportation and spreading of the raw manure. Because of the higher nitrogen content in the raw manure, a higher amount of synthetic fertilizers could additionally be substituted. Hence, the net effect of the no-treatment scenario is a potential fossil fuel saving. This finding is also reported by ten Hoeve et al. (2014); Brockmann et al. (2014); De Vries et al. (2012); and Lopez-Ridaura et al. (2009).
35: The net climate change impact of the treatment scenario is 46% lower than the no-treatment scenario. Although higher synthetic ferti- lizer amounts are displaced by the application of raw manure, this benefit could not compensate the damaging impact caused by the N2O emissions from field application of raw manure and the CH4 emission from raw manure storage. This is in accordance with Brockmann et al. (2014) and Lopez-Ridaura et al. (2009), who found that besides the emission from field application, the emissions during long-period sto- rage of raw manure in the no-treatment scenario dominate the climate change impact.
The net impact of potential photochemical oxidation of the

treatment scenario is 131% higher than that of the no-treatment. This is a result of the higher amounts of avoided NO emission from N&P synthetic fertilizer mix production in the no-treatment scenario. However, without the compensation for avoided synthetic fertilizer, the damage caused by the treatment scenario (6.79E-02kg NMVOC) is lower than the damage caused by the no-treatment one (1.21E-01 kg NMVOC). In both cases, the damaging impact is mainly induced by NO emissions during the transportation process, either of raw manure from the farm to the treatment plant or of raw manure to the agriculture field where it can be spread, a distance three times longer than in the treatment scenario.

The net particulate matter formation of the treatment scenario was one order of magnitude lower than the one in the no-treatment sce- nario. Increased NH3 emissions during field application of raw manure were the main source of this difference. The same trend was observed in the work of Brockmann et al. (2014). As the goal of the raw manure treatment is to reduce its nitrogen content by initrification-denitrifica- tion, NH3 emissions from field application of the treated manure pro- ducts (effluent and compost) are lower than from untreated raw manure. Even with a higher mitigation from the substitution of syn- thetic fertilizer utilisation in the no-treatment scenario, only 11% of the particulate matter formation from NH3 emissions during storage and field application of raw manure were mitigated by this substitution.

The damaging impacts caused by the treatment scenario to human toxicity and terrestrial ecotoxicity (2.28E + 01 kg 1.4-DB eq. and 2.26E-01 1.4-DB eq., respectively) are similar to those of the no- treatment scenario (2.55E + 01 kg 1.4-DB eq. and 2.75E-01 kg 1.4-DB eq., respectively) as in fact the biological treatment and composting processes do not remove heavy metals, which are responsible of 80%/ 98% human toxicity/terrestrial ecotoxicity damaging impact values. However, since effluent and compost have lower nitrogen contents than raw manure, the amount of avoided synthetic fertilizer utilisation and associated heavy metal emission in the no-treatment scenario is higher.

The net impact of freshwater eutrophication (only dealing with P- compounds) shows a negative value (damage mitigation) for the no- treatment and a positive value (damage) for the treatment scenario. However, the damage in the treatment scenario (4.52E-03 kg P) is lower than the damage caused in the no-treatment scenario (5.41E-03 kg P). In the treatment scenario, a large share of P separated into the solid fraction eventually becomes compost. This nutrient in the compost has been converted into a much more stable form (Bernal et al., 2009) and consequently compost has a lower phosphorus leaching potential in comparison to raw manure. But it also has a lower work efficiency compared to P in manure products. This explains the difference in net damage between the two scenarios, as a lower amount of synthetic P fertilizer mix can be substituted in the treatment scenario.

The treatment scenario shows a 53% lower net potential marine eutrophication impact than the no-treatment. This in spite of the fact that only 22% of the marine eutrophication damaging impact in the treatment scenario is counteracted by the substitution of synthetic fertilizers, in comparison to 34% of the damage prevented in the no- treatment scenario. However, the damaging impact in the no-treatment scenario (3.41E-01 kg eq.), which originates mainly from nitrate leaching and ammonia emissions during the field application of manure, more than doubles the damaging impact from the treatment scenario (1.35E-01 N eq.), which was mainly induced by electricity consumption. Similarly, Brockmann et al. (2014) reported that the total marine eutrophication potential from the no-treatment is twice as high as that of the treatment scenario. ten Hoeve et al. (2014) reported that the manure treatment with centrifugation gave a 31% lower net marine eutrophication impact. To the contrary, Lopez-Ridaura et al. (2009) found a higher eutrophication potential in the treatment scenario be- cause of the high NH3 emission during the biological treatment, whilst the biological treatment in our study is considered to have low NH3 emissions (Smet et al., 2003). Thus, the treatment scenario scores better in terms of marine eutrophication, as the net damage caused is less than
half of that in the no-treatment scenario.

The net impact on terrestrial acidification was 95% lower for the treatment scenario than for the no-treatment one. The emission of NH3 from the field application is the most considerable contributor to this category. In the treatment scenario, NH3 emission is very small because: (1) the NH3 emission from the raw manure storage in the treatment plant is assumed to be zero as it occurs in a closed system; (2) the NH3 emission during biological treatment is very low (2% of the total im- pact) due to optimum nitrification conditions in the aerobic reactor (Smet et al., 2003); (3) the composting process occurs in a closed tunnel, hence, the gaseous emissions during the composting process were deemed to be zero (Lemmens et al., 2007); and (4) most of the nitrogen content ends up in the liquid fraction after centrifugation, which is then followed by the biological treatment with efficient ni- trogen removal. Likewise, Brockmann et al. (2014) and Lopez-Ridaura et al. (2009) found that manure treatment has a better environmental performance than no-treatment in terms of potential terrestrial acid- ification, even though the difference between the two scenarios is not SO large since emission from slurry storage is included, and the emission from their biological treatment is higher.

The treatment scenario caused a higher agricultural land occupation especially due to the higher energy consumption through electricity for the treatment plant operations and the composting process. In the no- treatment scenario, the only processes that consume energy are the transportation to and the spreading of raw manure on the cropland, two processes with a low impact on agricultural land occupation.

For urban land occupation, the damaging impact caused by the treatment scenario is 32% lower than the no-treatment scenario mainly because of the transportation processes involved. Even though both scenarios have similar loads, the distance travelled in the no-treatment scenario is more than three times that in the treatment scenario. While both scenarios result in actual urban land occupation impact remedia- tion, the net remediation in the no-treatment scenario is more than two times larger than the one of the treatment scenario. This mainly thanks to the higher fertilizer substitution in the no-treatment scenario.

The treatment scenario poses higher fossil fuel depletion damaging impact than the no-treatment scenario (41% higher). In the treatment scenario, the energy demand is primarily needed for the provision of electricity, chemical products and transportation. The fossil fuel de- pletion impact from the Belgian medium voltage electricity production mix, required to support the biological treatment and the composting processes, is mainly due to the utilisation of natural gas (50%) and coal (16%) as energy sources (Weidema et al., 2013). In contrast, the fossil fuel consumption of the no-treatment scenario is only associated with field operations such as transportation and spreading of the raw manure. Because of the higher nitrogen content in the raw manure, a higher amount of synthetic fertilizers could additionally be substituted. Hence, the net effect of the no-treatment scenario is a potential fossil fuel saving. This finding is also reported by ten Hoeve et al. (2014); Brockmann et al. (2014); De Vries et al. (2012); and Lopez-Ridaura et al. (2009).

37: In this study, the treatment scenario had a better environmental performance (difference > 10%) than the no-treatment scenario in five out of eighteen midpoint impact categories (climate change, terrestrial acidification, marine eutrophication, particulate matter formation and terrestrial ecotoxicity-performing worse in the rest of categories) and two out of three endpoint impact categories (human health and eco- system diversity). At first view, the no-treatment scenario might be the better option for manure management, but this requires further dis- cussion. It has to be considered that some impacts act locally (e.g. terrestrial acidification), and others act globally (e.g. climate change). This will affect the choice between the two scenarios. Ideally, LCA would pinpoint an overall score, where all impact categories were

included in one single number. This requires normalization weighting of the individual impact categories and may differ among stakeholders. Nonetheless, the objective of comparing manure management scenarios is to provide solid scientific basis for the environmental consequences of choosing either option. LCA is not well enough developed to present a clear conclusion yet (Schaubroeck et al., 2015), and uncertainties in emissions add to this reserve (see Section 3.6). Further research is needed to improve LCA and to also consider social and economic tra- deoffs (Schaubroeck and Rugani, 2017).

From an environmental point of view, there are no regional boundaries in LCA studies. Global scale effects (e.g. global warming) are considered and this makes the discussion of local burdens somehow difficult. In the case of the no-treatment scenario, the environmental impact abatement arises from the avoided synthetic fertilizer produc- tion, transportation, application and emissions. Nevertheless, no eva- luation was carried out on whether there is enough cropland to apply this amount of nutrients (given the transportation distance considered), or how the transportation distance would have to be increased to find land were the manure can be applied without a heavy local environ- mental burden. This would require to make an assessment of not only one manure installation, but also of the entire manure production in a region and its croplands, and the nutrient requirements in croplands in the vicinity. Recently application of LCA to territories or regions have been introduced (Loiseau et al., 2014) and could be applied.

In general, a global management with effective nutrient surplus management and closing mineral/nutrient cycles would be the best environmental option given that the environmental damage required to make this happen does not out-scale the benefits. In areas with poor water quality or with a too high nutrient imbalance, such as the Nitrate Vulnerable Zones in Europe, treatment is necessary. Improvements could then be achieved by the use of innovative nitrogen removal processes, e.g. partial initritation-anammox (Kartal et al., 2010) and denitrification over nitrite (Al-Omari et al., 2015). These processes require lower aeration/energy (improving impacts in climate change, fossil depletion, marine eutrophication and agricultural land occupation) and would also lower (or even eliminate) the need for external carbon dosage. Anaerobic treatment of manure resulting in bioenergy has also been seen as a key target for environmental performance improvements in pig production (McAuliffe et al., 2016). Furthermore, using the nitrogen in wastewater to produce feed (in the form of microbial protein) instead of its removal as nitrogen gas (Matassa et al., 2015) has also been proposed to have a more efficient use of resources.

The evaluation of the two manure management options (treatment and no-treatment) presented focuses on their environmental sustain- ability. Nevertheless, other aspects can be accounted (e.g. socio-eco- nomic), and different ways of processing the impact values can be used, such as single-score assessments (see Section 3.5). Regarding socio- economic impacts, for example, one could argue that thanks to the treatment of manure, the pig industry has been able to stay (and even expand) in Belgium; an economic activity which had an economic production value above 1200 million euro since 2000 (DPM, 2017). Nonetheless, there is still a lot of social debate on whether this is the correct policy to follow (Vanden Bussche, 2017). For farmers, failing to fulfill their manure processing obligation results in administrative fines of 2 euro per kg N which has not been processed, increasing to 4 euro if there is another violation within 5 years (VCM, 2017). The treatment of manure has shown to be an option for farmers, as its use has risen in the last years (2013-2015) while the export of raw manure has stagnated (VCM, 2015). Results are quite specific for a country/region, as the life cycle may differ (e.g. different electricity production) and other reg- ulations may be in place. Care must hence be taken in using these re- sults for other regions/countries.
3.4. Sensitivity to electricity mix and transportation system
Electricity consumption and transportation have an important role
Fig. 4. Midpoint damaging impact reductions by changing: (gray) transportation system from EURO 3 to EURO 6, and (black) Belgium electricity mix to label-certified electricity mix - renewable energy.

in the environmental performance of manure management (see Section 3.3 and Fig. 3). It is therefore interesting to evaluate the effect of changing: 1) the electricity production system to renewable sources (Belgian medium voltage electricity production to Swiss medium vol- tage, label-certified electricity production mix, as specified in ecoinvent v3.2), and 2) the transportation system to one with better emission standards (from EURO 3 emission class to EURO 6).

From the 11 midpoint categories pinpointed in Section 3.1, six show a considerable sensitivity to the electricity sources used to supply the manure management practice (i.e. a change higher than 10% in the damaging impact): climate change, freshwater eutrophication, marine eutrophication, photochemical oxidant formation, agricultural land occupation, and fossil depletion (Fig. 4). This shows the importance of regional considerations when selecting background-processes inventory data. Furthermore, when an LCA study is to be used for policy making, not only possible changes to the foreground system (Fig. 1) should be considered; an analysis of this system together with the visions and perspectives for background processes must be evaluated.

The change to EURO 6 improved mainly 4 categories: climate change, photochemical oxidant formation, particulate matter forma- tion, and fossil depletion. Comparisons between these two improve- ments (electricity mix and transportation system) are hard and require further improvements in LCA methodologies. As discussed in Section 3.3, one way of making this type of comparisons is through a single score analysis (see Section 3.5).
It is important to notice that only the categories pinpointed in Section 3.1 are displayed in Fig. 4. The complete table with all 18 impact categories can be found in Table S6.1.

Nonetheless, there is still a lot of social debate on whether this is the correct policy to follow (Vanden Bussche, 2017). For farmers, failing to fulfill their manure processing obligation results in administrative fines of 2 euro per kg N which has not been processed, increasing to 4 euro if there is another violation within 5 years (VCM, 2017). The treatment of manure has shown to be an option for farmers, as its use has risen in the last years (2013-2015) while the export of raw manure has stagnated (VCM, 2015). Results are quite specific for a country/region, as the life cycle may differ (e.g. different electricity production) and other reg- ulations may be in place. Care must hence be taken in using these re- sults for other regions/countries.
3.4. Sensitivity to electricity mix and transportation system
Electricity consumption and transportation have an important role
Fig. 4. Midpoint damaging impact reductions by changing: (gray) transportation system from EURO 3 to EURO 6, and (black) Belgium electricity mix to label-certified electricity mix - renewable energy.

in the environmental performance of manure management (see Section 3.3 and Fig. 3). It is therefore interesting to evaluate the effect of changing: 1) the electricity production system to renewable sources (Belgian medium voltage electricity production to Swiss medium vol- tage, label-certified electricity production mix, as specified in ecoinvent v3.2), and 2) the transportation system to one with better emission standards (from EURO 3 emission class to EURO 6).

From the 11 midpoint categories pinpointed in Section 3.1, six show a considerable sensitivity to the electricity sources used to supply the manure management practice (i.e. a change higher than 10% in the damaging impact): climate change, freshwater eutrophication, marine eutrophication, photochemical oxidant formation, agricultural land occupation, and fossil depletion (Fig. 4). This shows the importance of regional considerations when selecting background-processes inventory data. Furthermore, when an LCA study is to be used for policy making, not only possible changes to the foreground system (Fig. 1) should be considered; an analysis of this system together with the visions and perspectives for background processes must be evaluated.

The change to EURO 6 improved mainly 4 categories: climate change, photochemical oxidant formation, particulate matter forma- tion, and fossil depletion. Comparisons between these two improve- ments (electricity mix and transportation system) are hard and require further improvements in LCA methodologies. As discussed in Section 3.3, one way of making this type of comparisons is through a single score analysis (see Section 3.5).
It is important to notice that only the categories pinpointed in Section 3.1 are displayed in Fig. 4. The complete table with all 18 impact categories can be found in Table S6.1.
40: In this study, the treatment scenario had a better environmental performance (difference > 10%) than the no-treatment scenario in five out of eighteen midpoint impact categories (climate change, terrestrial acidification, marine eutrophication, particulate matter formation and terrestrial ecotoxicity-performing worse in the rest of categories) and two out of three endpoint impact categories (human health and eco- system diversity). At first view, the no-treatment scenario might be the better option for manure management, but this requires further dis- cussion. It has to be considered that some impacts act locally (e.g. terrestrial acidification), and others act globally (e.g. climate change). This will affect the choice between the two scenarios. Ideally, LCA would pinpoint an overall score, where all impact categories were

included in one single number. This requires normalization weighting of the individual impact categories and may differ among stakeholders. Nonetheless, the objective of comparing manure management scenarios is to provide solid scientific basis for the environmental consequences of choosing either option. LCA is not well enough developed to present a clear conclusion yet (Schaubroeck et al., 2015), and uncertainties in emissions add to this reserve (see Section 3.6). Further research is needed to improve LCA and to also consider social and economic tra- deoffs (Schaubroeck and Rugani, 2017).

From an environmental point of view, there are no regional boundaries in LCA studies. Global scale effects (e.g. global warming) are considered and this makes the discussion of local burdens somehow difficult. In the case of the no-treatment scenario, the environmental impact abatement arises from the avoided synthetic fertilizer produc- tion, transportation, application and emissions. Nevertheless, no eva- luation was carried out on whether there is enough cropland to apply this amount of nutrients (given the transportation distance considered), or how the transportation distance would have to be increased to find land were the manure can be applied without a heavy local environ- mental burden. This would require to make an assessment of not only one manure installation, but also of the entire manure production in a region and its croplands, and the nutrient requirements in croplands in the vicinity. Recently application of LCA to territories or regions have been introduced (Loiseau et al., 2014) and could be applied.

In general, a global management with effective nutrient surplus management and closing mineral/nutrient cycles would be the best environmental option given that the environmental damage required to make this happen does not out-scale the benefits. In areas with poor water quality or with a too high nutrient imbalance, such as the Nitrate Vulnerable Zones in Europe, treatment is necessary. Improvements could then be achieved by the use of innovative nitrogen removal processes, e.g. partial initritation-anammox (Kartal et al., 2010) and denitrification over nitrite (Al-Omari et al., 2015). These processes require lower aeration/energy (improving impacts in climate change, fossil depletion, marine eutrophication and agricultural land occupation) and would also lower (or even eliminate) the need for external carbon dosage. Anaerobic treatment of manure resulting in bioenergy has also been seen as a key target for environmental performance improvements in pig production (McAuliffe et al., 2016). Furthermore, using the nitrogen in wastewater to produce feed (in the form of microbial protein) instead of its removal as nitrogen gas (Matassa et al., 2015) has also been proposed to have a more efficient use of resources.

The evaluation of the two manure management options (treatment and no-treatment) presented focuses on their environmental sustain- ability. Nevertheless, other aspects can be accounted (e.g. socio-eco- nomic), and different ways of processing the impact values can be used, such as single-score assessments (see Section 3.5). Regarding socio- economic impacts, for example, one could argue that thanks to the treatment of manure, the pig industry has been able to stay (and even expand) in Belgium; an economic activity which had an economic production value above 1200 million euro since 2000 (DPM, 2017). Nonetheless, there is still a lot of social debate on whether this is the correct policy to follow (Vanden Bussche, 2017). For farmers, failing to fulfill their manure processing obligation results in administrative fines of 2 euro per kg N which has not been processed, increasing to 4 euro if there is another violation within 5 years (VCM, 2017). The treatment of manure has shown to be an option for farmers, as its use has risen in the last years (2013-2015) while the export of raw manure has stagnated (VCM, 2015). Results are quite specific for a country/region, as the life cycle may differ (e.g. different electricity production) and other reg- ulations may be in place. Care must hence be taken in using these re- sults for other regions/countries.

in the environmental performance of manure management (see Section 3.3 and Fig. 3). It is therefore interesting to evaluate the effect of changing: 1) the electricity production system to renewable sources (Belgian medium voltage electricity production to Swiss medium vol- tage, label-certified electricity production mix, as specified in ecoinvent v3.2), and 2) the transportation system to one with better emission standards (from EURO 3 emission class to EURO 6).

From the 11 midpoint categories pinpointed in Section 3.1, six show a considerable sensitivity to the electricity sources used to supply the manure management practice (i.e. a change higher than 10% in the damaging impact): climate change, freshwater eutrophication, marine eutrophication, photochemical oxidant formation, agricultural land occupation, and fossil depletion (Fig. 4). This shows the importance of regional considerations when selecting background-processes inventory data. Furthermore, when an LCA study is to be used for policy making, not only possible changes to the foreground system (Fig. 1) should be considered; an analysis of this system together with the visions and perspectives for background processes must be evaluated.

The change to EURO 6 improved mainly 4 categories: climate change, photochemical oxidant formation, particulate matter forma- tion, and fossil depletion. Comparisons between these two improve- ments (electricity mix and transportation system) are hard and require further improvements in LCA methodologies. As discussed in Section 3.3, one way of making this type of comparisons is through a single score analysis (see Section 3.5).
It is important to notice that only the categories pinpointed in Section 3.1 are displayed in Fig. 4. The complete table with all 18 impact categories can be found in Table S6.1.
45: <header>3.5. Single score analysis and policy decisions </header>

As discussed in Section 3.3 and 3.6, single score analysis can provide a clear message for policy-oriented measures. Nevertheless, one must keep in mind how the normalization and weighting is done (which impact category is considered more important than the other) when interpreting the results.

As an example, the midpoint impact results were translated into a single score by normalizing them with the yearly impact of a European citizen as a reference (Europe Recipe H Normalization-Weighting) When applying this normalization and weighting, the no-treatment scenario had an impact score (0.186) that is 79% higher (higher en- vironmental impact) than that of the treatment scenario (0.104). This would pinpoint the treatment scenario as more environmentally friendly but this message should be interpreted with care. Fig. 5 shows the higher contributions to the scores of both the treatment and the no- treatment scenarios.
Fig. 5. Single-score analysis weighting according to the yearly emission of a European citizen.

These results give a clear view of which impact categories play major roles in the performance of both manure management strategies. However, in this case, this is only considering how the management compares to the average European, but can be questioned to really value how important one category is compared to the other (Schaubroeck et al., 2015; Dahlbo et al., 2013). In our case, for ex- ample, one could say that moving towards renewable power sources (see Section 3.4), would be a good policy. If renewable electricity is used (instead of the Belgian electricity mix), the single score is reduced by 26% to 0.077. This, in comparison to a 11% reduction (to 0.092) when improving the transportation system.

Weighting impact categories net results into a single score can be an appropriate way of translating LCA results into policy-oriented decision making (Brilhuis-Meijer, 2017). Nonetheless, another layer of un- certainty enters as each study must choose the basis of weighting, e.g. policy targets, scientific targets, monetization, etc. For the time being, it is believed that further research is required to achieve proper single- score analyses (Schaubroeck and Rugani, 2017).

48: In this study, the biological removal of nitrogen from the thin fraction of manure after mechanical separation was evaluated as it is the most applied technic in Flanders, Belgium (VLM, 2015). Future research could also consider other technics, such as: co-digestion, drying/thermal hygienization, bio-thermal drying, combinations with wetlands, etc. (VCM, 2014).
49: A study regarding the transportation distance required in order to find land where the manure can be applied without a heavy environ- mental burden of application and transportation could provide further insights in the limitations and challenges within circular nutrient management, and its costs and benefits.
50: For the treatment scenario, no emissions were considered from the underground raw manure storage, the settling tank and the effluent lagoon, which can be important -especially for climate change (Brockmann et al., 2014). The main emissions expected from the un- derground storage are ammonia and methane. Both of these emissions

depend directly on the storage temperature and time (shorter storage means lower emissions). Ammonia emissions would be mainly driven by the surface area of the storage, while methane by the stored volume (Lopez-Ridaura et al., 2009). For the settling tank and effluent lagoon, methane and nitrous oxide emissions are expected to play a role in the environmental performance of the process. In these two tanks methane is expected to have a lower impact than the one generated during the underground storage, as lower emission factors are seen in literature (e.g. Brockmann et al., 2014). Therefore, the main focus should be in obtaining accurate factors for ammonia and methane emissions during raw manure storage in order to have more accurate LCA results for the studied system and in general the storage period should be minimized to abate these emissions. Furthermore, an approach to account for ni- trate runoff to surface waters needs to be developed and considered in future studies.
51: For the treatment scenario, no emissions were considered from the underground raw manure storage, the settling tank and the effluent lagoon, which can be important -especially for climate change (Brockmann et al., 2014). The main emissions expected from the un- derground storage are ammonia and methane. Both of these emissions

depend directly on the storage temperature and time (shorter storage means lower emissions). Ammonia emissions would be mainly driven by the surface area of the storage, while methane by the stored volume (Lopez-Ridaura et al., 2009). For the settling tank and effluent lagoon, methane and nitrous oxide emissions are expected to play a role in the environmental performance of the process. In these two tanks methane is expected to have a lower impact than the one generated during the underground storage, as lower emission factors are seen in literature (e.g. Brockmann et al., 2014). Therefore, the main focus should be in obtaining accurate factors for ammonia and methane emissions during raw manure storage in order to have more accurate LCA results for the studied system and in general the storage period should be minimized to abate these emissions. Furthermore, an approach to account for ni- trate runoff to surface waters needs to be developed and considered in future studies.
52: In the current work, modelling has been used to describe the bio- logical treatment of the thin fraction of manure. Predictions regarding ammonia, nitrite and nitrate in the effluent have been made. For these instances, modelling has proven accurate for the dynamic prediction of biological treatment of heavy loaded wastewater (Ganigué et al., 2010; Tilche et al., 2001; Magrí and Flotats, 2008). In contrast, a precise prediction of N2O emissions through modelling remains a challenge (Mampaey, 2016). Since N2O is a crucial element in environmental impact assessment of manure treatment processes (Brockmann et al., 2014; Lopez-Ridaura et al., 2009), this poses a challenge for coupling LCA and modelling of manure management scenarios.

Emission factors could alternatively be used to assess N2O emissions in LCA studies, but it is important acknowledge the large uncertainties in this respect. High variability in these factors has been observed de- pending on the sampling strategy used to obtain them (Daelman et al., 2013). Short-term sampling turned out to be inadequate to accurately estimate average N2O emissions from a particular WWTP. The approach taken for the assessment of N2O emissions in LCA studies will depend on the available resources, the aim, and the desired accuracy. Un- certainties in N2O as well CH4 emission values can have an important impact on climate change impact calculations (De Vries et al., 2015).

54: As discussed in Sections 3.1-3.3, electricity was found to be one of the main drivers of environmental impact in the treatment scenario. A correct electricity consumption estimation is thus important to obtain reliable LCA results. When one aims to link modelling with an LCA study and one wants to consider the effect of different (novel) aeration strategies (low aeration, different diffusers, bubble size distribution, etc.), advanced aeration modelling (Amaral et al., 2016) might be an option. The coupling of advanced aeration modelling and biological treatment modelling remains a challenge and needs further investiga- tion.
55: As discussed in the previous sections and further observed by Brockmann et al. (2014), emissions from storage, composting, and land application play a considerable role in the impact assessment. If a fra- mework is to be developed to include a beginning-to-end modelling of a treatment scenario, an interphase between each process step needs to be included. When accurate mechanistic models are used, uncertainties of emission factors can be reduced by considering specific circum- stances for each case under study. Furthermore, the use of models could enable the inclusion of different feeds, animal performance, housing, treatment practices, etc. (Rigolot et al., 2010a,b).
56: In this study, steady-state values have been used to account for the environmental performance of the studied scenarios. Nevertheless,

nutrient and flow peaks can also impact LCA results (de Faria et al., 2015). Furthermore, this type of modelling can be used to check the viability of the different management schemes to be considered, this in terms of operation stability, effluent quality, etc. Dynamic modelling and LCA can be coupled through the creation of an interface, as shown by de Faria et al. (2015), using a Python™ interface. This will allow an LCA evaluation that considers realistic dynamic values during opera- tion, average plant performance and emissions, and direct foreground and background processes. Similar research on manure treatment could be applied in the future.
57: The hotspots dominating the environmental impact for manure treatment were identified using life cycle assessment:

<list>1. Field application of compost: dominates terrestrial acidification and with large impacts on particulate matter formation, terrestrial eco- toxicity, human toxicity and freshwater eutrophication. 
2. Field application of effluent: dominates terrestrial ecotoxicity and human toxicity. 
3. Electricity consumption: dominates agricultural land occupation and marine eutrophication, and with large impacts on climate change and fossil fuel depletion.</list>

The potential substitution of synthetic fertilizers plays an important role in the impact of both scenarios considered. For the treatment scenario, this substitution mitigated more than 50% of the potential damage regarding climate change, human toxicity, freshwater eu- trophication and urban land occupation. For the no-treatment scenario, this degree of mitigation occurred in most categories, except particular matter formation, terrestrial ecotoxicity, marine eutrophication and terrestrial acidification.

Manure treatment accomplishes its goal in preventing marine eu- trophication (related to nitrogen containing nutrients) and it must be carried out in nitrate-vulnerable zones. It is further important to con- sider novel strategies within the manure management context, e.g. more environmentally friendly synthetic fertilizer production, revise/ refine regulation with respect to manure application, treatment with energy/nutrient recovery.

The comparison between the treatment scenario and the no-treat- ment scenario could not straight-forwardly conclude which one had the better environmental performance. In order to achieve better results to support decision making, impacts at midpoint could be combined into one final score using normalisation and weighing factors depending on the questions raised. Nevertheless, current literature has not provided a considerable sound approach for that, in our opinion.

Modelling can be a powerful tool in supporting LCA studies that aim to evaluate different process configurations. Once hotspots have been identified, special attention should be paid to correctly predict the processes/substances that are related to these hotspots (e.g. electricity for aeration, N20 emissions, etc.).
